**Manchester**[[a]](#cite_note-7) is a city in [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester"), England. It had a population of over 589,000 in 2024. It borders the [Cheshire Plain](/wiki/Cheshire_Plain "Cheshire Plain") to the south, the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") to the north and east, and the city of [Salford](/wiki/City_of_Salford "City of Salford") to the west. The two cities and the surrounding towns form one of the United Kingdom's most populous [conurbations](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation"), the [Greater Manchester Built-up Area](/wiki/Greater_Manchester_Built-up_Area "Greater Manchester Built-up Area"), which has a population of 2.87 million.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The [history of Manchester](/wiki/History_of_Manchester "History of Manchester") began with the civilian settlement associated with the [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain") fort of *Mamucium* or *Mancunium*, established around AD 79 on a sandstone bluff near the confluence of the rivers [Medlock](/wiki/River_Medlock "River Medlock") and [Irwell](/wiki/River_Irwell "River Irwell"). [Historically](/wiki/Historic_counties_of_England "Historic counties of England") part of [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"), areas of [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire") south of the [River Mersey](/wiki/River_Mersey "River Mersey") were incorporated into Manchester in the 20th century. Throughout the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") Manchester remained a [manorial](/wiki/Manorialism "Manorialism") township, but began to expand significantly with a boom in [textile manufacture during the Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Textile_manufacture_during_the_Industrial_Revolution "Textile manufacture during the Industrial Revolution"),[[8]](#cite_note-Cotton-9) which resulted in it becoming the world's first industrialised city.[[9]](#cite_note-Industrial_city-10) Manchester attained [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") in 1853. The [Manchester Ship Canal](/wiki/Manchester_Ship_Canal "Manchester Ship Canal") opened in 1894, creating the [Port of Manchester](/wiki/Port_of_Manchester "Port of Manchester") and linking the city to the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea"), 36 miles (58 km) to the west. Its fortune declined after the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), owing to deindustrialisation, and the [1996 Manchester bombing](/wiki/1996_Manchester_bombing "1996 Manchester bombing") led to extensive investment and regeneration.[[10]](#cite_note-Williams_2016-11)

Following considerable redevelopment, Manchester was the host city for the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games"). The city is notable for [its architecture](/wiki/Architecture_of_Manchester "Architecture of Manchester"), [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Manchester "Culture of Manchester"), [musical exports](/wiki/Popular_music_of_Manchester "Popular music of Manchester"), [media links](/wiki/Media_in_Manchester "Media in Manchester"), [scientific and engineering output](/wiki/Science_and_engineering_in_Manchester "Science and engineering in Manchester"), [social impact](/wiki/Sociology_of_Manchester "Sociology of Manchester"), [sports clubs](/wiki/Sport_in_Manchester "Sport in Manchester") and [transport connections](/wiki/Transport_in_Manchester "Transport in Manchester").

## Toponymy

The name *Manchester* originates from **[Mamucium](/wiki/Mamucium "Mamucium")**, the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name for the city, or its variant **Mancunio**; its citizens are still referred to as Mancunians.[[b]](#cite_note-12) These names are generally thought to represent a [Latinised](/wiki/Latinization_of_names "Latinization of names") version of an older [Brittonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic") name. It is generally accepted that the etymology of the Brittonic name is from \***mamm*-*, which means '[breast](/wiki/Breast "Breast")', in reference to a [breast-shaped hill](/wiki/Breast-shaped_hill "Breast-shaped hill") on which the city was built.[[11]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Place_names-14) However, more recent work suggests that the name could have instead come from the Brittonic \***mamma**, which means 'mother', in reference to a [local river](/wiki/River_Medlock "River Medlock") goddess.[[13]](#cite_note-breeze-15)[[c]](#cite_note-16) The [suffix](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") *[-chester](/wiki/Chester_(placename_element) "Chester (placename element)")* is from [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") **ceaster** ('Roman fortification', itself a loanword from Latin **castra**, 'fort; fortified town'),[[11]](#cite_note-:0-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Place_names-14) and was first used after the [end of Roman rule in Britain](/wiki/End_of_Roman_rule_in_Britain "End of Roman rule in Britain") to describe places with former links to the Roman military.[[14]](#cite_note-17)

Nicknames for the city that originated from its role in the Industrial Revolution include "warehouse city" and "[cottonopolis](/wiki/Cottonopolis "Cottonopolis")". The city is widely known as 'the [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of the [North](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England")'[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19)[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21) and is part of an ongoing dispute with the larger city of [Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham "Birmingham") to be the unofficial [second city of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Second_city_of_the_United_Kingdom "Second city of the United Kingdom").[[19]](#cite_note-No_prizes-22)[[20]](#cite_note-auto-23)[[21]](#cite_note-New_Statesman-24)[[22]](#cite_note-25) The city is also infrequently referred to as 'Manny',[[23]](#cite_note-:16-26)[[24]](#cite_note-:17-27)[[25]](#cite_note-:18-28) especially by non-Mancunians, which is considered offensive by some residents.[[23]](#cite_note-:16-26) The phrase was particularly popularised by rapper [Bugzy Malone](/wiki/Bugzy_Malone "Bugzy Malone")'s use of the phrase "putting Manny on the map".[[24]](#cite_note-:17-27)[[25]](#cite_note-:18-28)

Although the name Manchester only officially applies to the [metropolitan borough](/wiki/Metropolitan_borough "Metropolitan borough") within the metropolitan county of [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester"),[[26]](#cite_note-29) it has been informally applied to various other areas over the years; examples include the "Manchester City Zone",[[27]](#cite_note-Rail-ticket-zone-map-30) "[Manchester post town](/wiki/M_postcode_area "M postcode area")",[[28]](#cite_note-address-management-guide-31) and the "[Manchester Congestion Charge](/wiki/Manchester_Congestion_Charge "Manchester Congestion Charge")",[[29]](#cite_note-appendix-1-32) none of which simply cover the official confines of the city.[[d]](#cite_note-33)

## History

Main article: [History of Manchester](/wiki/History_of_Manchester "History of Manchester")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Manchester history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Manchester_history "Timeline of Manchester history").

### Before 1066: Early history

The first major [Celtic tribe](/wiki/Celtic_tribes_in_Britain_and_Ireland "Celtic tribes in Britain and Ireland") in what is now [Northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England") were the [Brigantes](/wiki/Brigantes "Brigantes"); they had a stronghold in the locality at a sandstone [outcrop](/wiki/Outcrop "Outcrop") on which [Manchester Cathedral](/wiki/Manchester_Cathedral "Manchester Cathedral") now stands, opposite the [River Irwell](/wiki/River_Irwell "River Irwell"). Their territory extended across the fertile lowland of what is now [Salford](/wiki/Salford,_Greater_Manchester "Salford, Greater Manchester") and [Stretford](/wiki/Stretford "Stretford"). In 79 AD, following their [conquest of Britain](/wiki/Roman_conquest_of_Britain "Roman conquest of Britain"), the Roman general [Agricola](/wiki/Gnaeus_Julius_Agricola "Gnaeus Julius Agricola") ordered the construction of a [fort](/wiki/Castra "Castra") named [Mamucium](/wiki/Mamucium "Mamucium") to protect Roman interests in [Deva Victrix](/wiki/Deva_Victrix "Deva Victrix") (now [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester")) and [Eboracum](/wiki/Eboracum "Eboracum") (now [York](/wiki/York "York")).[[30]](#cite_note-Cooper-34) Central Manchester has remained a continuously populated settlement since.[[31]](#cite_note-Roman-35)

Fragments of the Mamucium fort remain visible in [Castlefield](/wiki/Castlefield "Castlefield"). The Roman habitation of Manchester probably ended around the 3rd century; its [civilian settlement](/wiki/Vicus_(Rome) "Vicus (Rome)") appears to have been abandoned by the mid-3rd century, although the fort may have supported a small garrison until the late 3rd or early 4th century.[[32]](#cite_note-36) The fort was first investigated by archaeologists in 1906,[[33]](#cite_note-37) and opened to the public in 1984.[[34]](#cite_note-guide-38)

### 1066–1800: Before industrialisation

[![A watercolour of mills with smoking chimneys painted from the opposite side of a river. There is a wooden dock on the left hand side with a small wooden boat by the dock, floating perpendicular to the river.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/McConnel_%26_Company_mills%2C_about_1820.jpg/250px-McConnel_%26_Company_mills%2C_about_1820.jpg)](/wiki/File:McConnel_%26_Company_mills,_about_1820.jpg)

McConnel & Company's [cotton mills](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill") in [Ancoats](/wiki/Ancoats "Ancoats"), c. 1820

After the Roman withdrawal and subsequent [Anglo-Saxon settlement](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlement "Anglo-Saxon settlement"), the centre of the town moved to the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of the rivers Irwell and [Irk](/wiki/River_Irk "River Irk").[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) In the Normans' [Harrying of the North](/wiki/Harrying_of_the_North "Harrying of the North"), much of the area surrounding Manchester was laid waste.[[36]](#cite_note-Hylton-40)[[37]](#cite_note-Arrowsmith-41) The [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") (1086) records Manchester within the [hundred of Salford](/wiki/Hundred_of_Salford "Hundred of Salford") and held as [tenant in chief](/wiki/Tenant_in_chief "Tenant in chief") by a Norman named [Roger of Poitou](/wiki/Roger_of_Poitou "Roger of Poitou").[[38]](#cite_note-doomsday-42) The town was later held by the Grelley family, who were the [lords of the manor](/wiki/Lord_of_the_manor "Lord of the manor") and residents of [Manchester Castle](/wiki/Manchester_Castle "Manchester Castle").[[39]](#cite_note-gatehouse-43) By 1421, Thomas de la Warre had founded a [collegiate church](/wiki/Collegiate_church "Collegiate church") for the [parish](/wiki/Manchester_(ancient_parish) "Manchester (ancient parish)"), which would later become [Manchester Cathedral](/wiki/Manchester_Cathedral "Manchester Cathedral"); other church buildings have since become [Chetham's School of Music](/wiki/Chetham%27s_School_of_Music "Chetham's School of Music") and [Chetham's Library](/wiki/Chetham%27s_Library "Chetham's Library").[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39)[[40]](#cite_note-Hartwell-44) The latter opened in 1653 and remains open to the public, the oldest free public reference library in the UK.[[41]](#cite_note-Nicholls2004P20-45)

Manchester is recorded as a [market](/wiki/Market_town "Market town") in 1282.[[42]](#cite_note-46) Around the 14th century, Manchester received an influx of [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people") weavers, which has sometimes been credited as the foundation of the region's textile industry.[[43]](#cite_note-Flemish-47) The town became an important centre for the manufacture and trade of [woollens](/wiki/Wool "Wool") and [linen](/wiki/Linen "Linen"), and by 1540 had expanded to become, in the words of [John Leland](/wiki/John_Leland_(antiquary) "John Leland (antiquary)"), "the fairest, best builded, quickest, and most populous town of all Lancashire".[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) The cathedral and Chetham's buildings are the only prominent survivors from that period.[[36]](#cite_note-Hylton-40)

During the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"), Manchester strongly favoured the Parliamentarians who were led by [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"). He gave the town the right to elect its [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)"); [Charles Worsley](/wiki/Charles_Worsley "Charles Worsley") was so elected and appointed as Major-General for Lancashire, Cheshire and Staffordshire during the [Rule of the Major-Generals](/wiki/Rule_of_the_Major-Generals "Rule of the Major-Generals"). A diligent [puritan](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan"), he shut down ale houses in the town and banned the celebration of Christmas.[[44]](#cite_note-48)

Large quantities of cotton were used after about 1600, firstly in linen and cotton [fustians](/wiki/Fustian "Fustian"); by around 1750, pure cotton fabrics were produced and cotton had overtaken wool in importance.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) The Irwell and Mersey were made navigable by 1736, opening a route from Manchester to the sea docks on the Mersey. The [Bridgewater Canal](/wiki/Bridgewater_Canal "Bridgewater Canal"), Britain's first wholly artificial waterway, opened in 1761, bringing coal from mines at [Worsley](/wiki/Worsley "Worsley") to central Manchester. The canal was extended to the Mersey by 1776. The combination of competition and improved efficiency halved the cost of coal and the transport cost of raw cotton.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39)[[40]](#cite_note-Hartwell-44) Manchester became the dominant marketplace for textiles produced in the surrounding towns.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) A [commodities exchange](/wiki/Commodities_exchange "Commodities exchange"), opened in 1729,[[36]](#cite_note-Hylton-40) and numerous large warehouses aided commerce. In 1780, [Richard Arkwright](/wiki/Richard_Arkwright "Richard Arkwright") began construction of Manchester's first cotton mill.[[36]](#cite_note-Hylton-40)[[40]](#cite_note-Hartwell-44) Manchester exported its cotton goods to Africa as a way of paying for slaves to be purchased for the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Transatlantic_slave_trade "Transatlantic slave trade");[[45]](#cite_note-:6-49) this supply line to Africa and its reliance on the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") supported Manchester's population and economic growth.[[45]](#cite_note-:6-49)[[46]](#cite_note-:7-50)

### 1800–1880: Industrialisation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/View_from_Kersal_Moor%2C_Salford_-_1820.jpg/250px-View_from_Kersal_Moor%2C_Salford_-_1820.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_Kersal_Moor,_Salford_-_1820.jpg)

*View from Kersal Moor* towards Manchester by [Sebastian Pether](/wiki/Sebastian_Pether "Sebastian Pether"), c. 1820, then still a rural landscape. Note the [River Irwell](/wiki/River_Irwell "River Irwell") in both paintings.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Wyld%2C_William_-_Manchester_from_Kersal_Moor%2C_with_rustic_figures_and_goats_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Wyld%2C_William_-_Manchester_from_Kersal_Moor%2C_with_rustic_figures_and_goats_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wyld,_William_-_Manchester_from_Kersal_Moor,_with_rustic_figures_and_goats_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Manchester from [Kersal Moor](/wiki/Kersal_Moor "Kersal Moor")*, by [William Wyld](/wiki/William_Wyld "William Wyld") in 1857, a view now dominated by chimney stacks as a consequence of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution")

Manchester was one of the centres of [textile manufacture during the Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Textile_manufacture_during_the_Industrial_Revolution "Textile manufacture during the Industrial Revolution"). The majority of [cotton spinning](/wiki/Spinning_(textiles) "Spinning (textiles)") took place in the towns of south Lancashire and north Cheshire, and Manchester was for a time the most productive centre of cotton processing.[[47]](#cite_note-GMArch-51) This caused the rapid expansion of the town that would lead to it becoming the world's first industrialised city.[[9]](#cite_note-Industrial_city-10) Manchester also became known as the world's largest marketplace for cotton goods;[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39)[[48]](#cite_note-Hall-52) it was dubbed "[Cottonopolis](/wiki/Cottonopolis "Cottonopolis")" and "Warehouse City" during the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era").[[47]](#cite_note-GMArch-51) Brought on by the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), there was rapid, unplanned urban expansion of Manchester at the turn of the 19th century.[[49]](#cite_note-53)[[50]](#cite_note-54)[[8]](#cite_note-Cotton-9) Engineering firms made machines for the cotton trade, then diversified into general manufacture. The chemical industry started by producing bleaches and dyes, but expanded into other areas. Commerce was supported by financial service industries such as banking and insurance.[[51]](#cite_note-55) In 1803, [John Dalton](/wiki/John_Dalton "John Dalton") formulated his atomic theory while teaching in the city.[[52]](#cite_note-56)

[![A coloured print depicting soldiers in blue and white uniforms amongst a crowd of people wearing various colours and waving unicolour flags. The people are fighting and some are on the floor.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Peterloo_Massacre.png/250px-Peterloo_Massacre.png)](/wiki/File:Peterloo_Massacre.png)

The [Peterloo Massacre](/wiki/Peterloo_Massacre "Peterloo Massacre") of 1819 resulted in 18 deaths and several hundred injured.

Manchester was the scene of bread and labour riots, as well as calls for greater political recognition by the city's working class.[[53]](#cite_note-:8-57) On 16 August 1819, large crowds protested in [St Peter's Square, Manchester](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Square,_Manchester "St Peter's Square, Manchester");[[54]](#cite_note-:9-58) estimates of the crowd range between 30,000–150,000 contemporaneously and 50,000–80,000 by modern critics.[[55]](#cite_note-59) When ordered to disperse the peaceful crowd, the soldiers charged them on horseback, killing at least 18 and injuring more than 700 in the [Peterloo Massacre](/wiki/Peterloo_Massacre "Peterloo Massacre").[[54]](#cite_note-:9-58)

The political landscape of early industrial Manchester contained capitalist and communist schools of thought.[[56]](#cite_note-:4-60) The city was the home of [Manchester Liberalism](/wiki/Manchester_Liberalism "Manchester Liberalism"), and the centre of the [Anti-Corn Law League](/wiki/Anti-Corn_Law_League "Anti-Corn Law League") after 1838.[[53]](#cite_note-:8-57) The city is the subject of [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels")' work *[The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1844](/wiki/The_Condition_of_the_Working_Class_in_England_in_1844 "The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1844"),* as Engels spent much of his life in and around Manchester[[57]](#cite_note-Engles-61) and met with [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") at [Chetham's Library](/wiki/Chetham%27s_Library "Chetham's Library").[[41]](#cite_note-Nicholls2004P20-45) The first [Trades Union Congress](/wiki/Trades_Union_Congress "Trades Union Congress") was held at the [Mechanics' Institute, Manchester](/wiki/Mechanics%27_Institute,_Manchester "Mechanics' Institute, Manchester") in 1868 and Manchester was an important centre of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), the [Suffragette](/wiki/Suffragette "Suffragette") Movement, and the [Chartist Movement](/wiki/Chartist_Movement "Chartist Movement").[[56]](#cite_note-:4-60)[[58]](#cite_note-62)

Trade, and feeding the growing population, required a large transport and distribution infrastructure: the canal system was extended, and Manchester became one end of the world's first intercity passenger railway – the [Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool and Manchester Railway") – in 1830.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) The number of [cotton mills](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill") in Manchester peaked at 108 in 1853; afterwards, the number declined and Manchester was surpassed as the largest centre of cotton spinning by [Bolton](/wiki/Bolton "Bolton") by the 1850s and [Oldham](/wiki/Oldham "Oldham") by the 1860s. This period of decline coincided with the rise of the city as the financial centre of the region.[[47]](#cite_note-GMArch-51) In 1878 the [General Post Office](/wiki/General_Post_Office "General Post Office") (the forerunner of [British Telecom](/wiki/BT_Group "BT Group")) provided its first telephones to a firm in Manchester.[[59]](#cite_note-63)

[![An oil painting depicting a smog-filled wide cobbled road with pavement on either side. In the background, a bridge goes over the road in front of a towering building to the right. In the foreground, there is a man walking and a small horse and cart moving away.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Oxford_Road%2C_Manchester_1910%2C_Valette.jpg/250px-Oxford_Road%2C_Manchester_1910%2C_Valette.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oxford_Road,_Manchester_1910,_Valette.jpg)

An oil painting of [Oxford Road](/wiki/Oxford_Road,_Manchester "Oxford Road, Manchester"), Manchester, in 1910, by [Valette](/wiki/Pierre_Adolphe_Valette "Pierre Adolphe Valette")

### 1880–1939: Impacts of industrialisation

New industrial processes were developed in the city, and the city had become known for its experimental ways of thinking: the [Manchester School](/wiki/Manchester_Liberalism "Manchester Liberalism") promoted [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") and *[laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire")*, there was the advent of new classes or groups in society and new religious sects, and the city was experimenting with new forms of labour organisation. The period saw the construction of some of its finest public buildings, including [Manchester Town Hall](/wiki/Manchester_Town_Hall "Manchester Town Hall"). It also enjoyed a vibrant culture, which included the [Hallé Orchestra](/wiki/Hall%C3%A9_Orchestra "Hallé Orchestra"). In 1889, when county councils were created in England, the municipal borough became a [county borough](/wiki/County_borough "County borough"), giving it greater autonomy.[[60]](#cite_note-GM_Gazetteer-64) Manchester was also a site of widespread poverty and squalor, with economic extremes on display throughout the city.[[61]](#cite_note-65)

The [Manchester Ship Canal](/wiki/Manchester_Ship_Canal "Manchester Ship Canal") was built between 1888 and 1894, in some sections by canalisation of the Rivers Irwell and Mersey, running 36 miles (58 km)[[62]](#cite_note-66) from [Salford](/wiki/Salford,_Greater_Manchester "Salford, Greater Manchester") to Eastham Locks on the tidal Mersey. This enabled oceangoing ships to sail into the Port of Manchester. On the canal's banks, just outside the borough, the world's first industrial estate was created at [Trafford Park](/wiki/Trafford_Park "Trafford Park").[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39)

Manchester continued to process cotton, consituting 65% of the world's production in 1913.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) The First World War interrupted access to the export markets; combined with increased cotton processing in other parts of the world, this led to the rapid decline of the city's textile industry.[[63]](#cite_note-67) Industry and employment suffered during the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"),[[64]](#cite_note-:10-68)[[65]](#cite_note-:11-69) particularly due to its effect on the value of British exports.[[64]](#cite_note-:10-68) Manchester also saw a cultural revolution in the 1930s as locals tried greater creativity and local pride to counteract the effect of the status of the economy; this included the first formation of the British [High Street](/wiki/High_Street "High Street"), and embarking on infrastructure projects such as the [Manchester Central Library](/wiki/Manchester_Central_Library "Manchester Central Library").[[65]](#cite_note-:11-69)

### 1939–1945: Second World War

[![An oil panting of people walking in all directions through a bleak square wearing a variety of colours but mostly all black. The square backs onto tall yellow buildings with telegraph cables stretched around. Part of a trolleybus is visible to the right-hand side.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Going_to_Work_-_L_S_Lowry.jpg/250px-Going_to_Work_-_L_S_Lowry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Going_to_Work_-_L_S_Lowry.jpg)

*Going to Work* by [L.S. Lowry](/wiki/L._S._Lowry "L. S. Lowry"), commissioned by the [War Artists' Advisory Committee](/wiki/War_Artists%27_Advisory_Committee "War Artists' Advisory Committee") in 1943

Manchester mobilised during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"): casting and machining expertise at [Beyer, Peacock & Company](/wiki/Beyer,_Peacock_%26_Company "Beyer, Peacock & Company")'s locomotive works in [Gorton](/wiki/Gorton "Gorton") was switched to bomb making and [Dunlop's](/wiki/Dunlop_Rubber "Dunlop Rubber") rubber works in [Chorlton-on-Medlock](/wiki/Chorlton-on-Medlock "Chorlton-on-Medlock") made [barrage balloons](/wiki/Barrage_balloon "Barrage balloon"). Manchester was the target of bombing by the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe"), and by late 1940 air raids were taking place against non-military targets.[[66]](#cite_note-:5-70)

The biggest air raids on the city during the war took place during the [Manchester Blitz](/wiki/Manchester_Blitz "Manchester Blitz") on the nights of 22–23 and 24–25 December 1940, when an estimated 474 tonnes (467 long tons) of high explosives and over 37,000 incendiary bombs were dropped. Much of the historic city centre was destroyed, including 165 warehouses, 200 business premises, and 150 offices. 376 were killed and 30,000 houses were damaged.[[66]](#cite_note-:5-70) [Manchester Cathedral](/wiki/Manchester_Cathedral "Manchester Cathedral"), [Royal Exchange](/wiki/Royal_Exchange,_Manchester "Royal Exchange, Manchester") and [Free Trade Hall](/wiki/Free_Trade_Hall "Free Trade Hall") were among the buildings seriously damaged, with the restoration of the cathedral taking 20 years.[[67]](#cite_note-WWII-71) 589 civilians were recorded to have died as a result of enemy action within the Manchester County Borough.[[68]](#cite_note-72)

### 1945–2000: Decline and regeneration

[![A photograph of a wide road from the right-hand pavement. Tall buildings tower on either side, with a white stucco building on the left and a red brick building on the right. A church tower is visible in the background in front of a church building.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Oxfordrd.jpg/250px-Oxfordrd.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oxfordrd.jpg)

[Oxford Road](/wiki/Wilmslow_Road "Wilmslow Road"), one of the main thoroughfares into [Manchester city centre](/wiki/Manchester_city_centre "Manchester city centre")

Cotton processing and trading continued to decline in peacetime, and the exchange closed in 1968.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) In 1963 the port of Manchester was the UK's third largest,[[69]](#cite_note-UK's_3rd_largest-73) and employed over 3,000 men, but the canal was unable to handle the increasingly large [container](/wiki/Containerization "Containerization") ships. Traffic declined, and the port closed in 1982.[[70]](#cite_note-ship_close-74) Heavy industry suffered a downturn from the 1960s and was greatly reduced under the economic policies of [Margaret Thatcher](/wiki/Margaret_Thatcher "Margaret Thatcher")'s government after 1979. Manchester lost 150,000 jobs in manufacturing between 1961 and 1983.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) Regeneration began in the late 1980s, with initiatives such as the [Metrolink](/wiki/Manchester_Metrolink "Manchester Metrolink"), the [Bridgewater Concert Hall](/wiki/Bridgewater_Hall "Bridgewater Hall") and the [Manchester Arena](/wiki/Manchester_Arena "Manchester Arena"). Two bids to host the Olympic Games were part of a process to raise the international profile of the city.[[71]](#cite_note-Regeneration-75)

[![A street covered in debris. On one side is a building whose side is half-destroyed, leaving only the steel frame. The road is wet and there is crime scene tap across the centre of the photo.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/8/87/BBC_picture_Arndale_centre_after_1996_bomb.jpg/250px-BBC_picture_Arndale_centre_after_1996_bomb.jpg)](/wiki/File:BBC_picture_Arndale_centre_after_1996_bomb.jpg)

[Corporation Street](/wiki/Corporation_Street,_Manchester "Corporation Street, Manchester") after [the bombing](/wiki/1996_Manchester_bombing "1996 Manchester bombing") in 1996

On 15 June 1996, the [Provisional Irish Republican Army](/wiki/Provisional_Irish_Republican_Army "Provisional Irish Republican Army") (IRA) set off a [lorry bomb](/wiki/Car_bomb "Car bomb") in [Corporation Street](/wiki/Corporation_Street,_Manchester "Corporation Street, Manchester") in the city centre. The largest to be detonated on British soil, the bomb injured over 200 people, heavily damaged buildings, and broke windows 1⁄2 mile (800 m) away.[[72]](#cite_note-1996_IRA_costs-76) It was one of the most expensive man-made disasters in history:[[73]](#cite_note-77) the cost of the immediate damage was initially estimated at £50 million (equivalent to £96,000,000 in 2023[[74]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-78)), but this was quickly revised upwards.[[72]](#cite_note-1996_IRA_costs-76) The final insurance pay-out was over £400 million (equivalent to £768,000,000 in 2023[[74]](#cite_note-inflation-UK-78)); many affected businesses never recovered from the loss of trade.[[75]](#cite_note-IRA_business-79) However, it is also credited as helping to drive the regeneration of the city.[[10]](#cite_note-Williams_2016-11)

### 2000–present: Modern day

[![A black-and-white ink drawing of a grand building stretching across the photo with a white tower in the corner. People are walking on the very wide street in front.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Corn%2C_Grocery_%26_Produce_Exchange%2C_Manchester%2C_c.1902.jpg/250px-Corn%2C_Grocery_%26_Produce_Exchange%2C_Manchester%2C_c.1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corn,_Grocery_%26_Produce_Exchange,_Manchester,_c.1902.jpg)

[![A partial view of the previous image but now a modern colour photograph; the building is a limestone facade and in the central tower there is a sign that says "CORN / EXCH / ANGE"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Corn_exchange_far_loz.JPG/250px-Corn_exchange_far_loz.JPG)](/wiki/File:Corn_exchange_far_loz.JPG)

The [Corn Exchange](/wiki/Corn_Exchange,_Manchester "Corn Exchange, Manchester") in 1902 (top) and 2013 (bottom)

Spurred by the investment after the 1996 bombing and aided by the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games"), the city centre underwent extensive regeneration.[[71]](#cite_note-Regeneration-75) [The Printworks](/wiki/The_Printworks_(Manchester) "The Printworks (Manchester)")[[76]](#cite_note-80) was redeveloped by architects [RTKL Associates](/wiki/RTKL_Associates "RTKL Associates") and reopened as a [leisure centre](/wiki/Leisure_centre "Leisure centre") and [cinema](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater").[[77]](#cite_note-MEN2-81)[[78]](#cite_note-82) The [Corn Exchange](/wiki/Corn_Exchange,_Manchester "Corn Exchange, Manchester")[[79]](#cite_note-hist1950-83) reopened as the Triangle Shopping Centre[[80]](#cite_note-84) and redeveloped in 2012.[[81]](#cite_note-85) [Manchester Arndale](/wiki/Manchester_Arndale "Manchester Arndale") is the UK's largest city-centre shopping centre.[[82]](#cite_note-Arndale-86)

Large parts of the city have been demolished, re-developed or modernised with the use of glass and steel. Former mills have been converted into apartments. The 47-storey, 554-foot (169 m) [Beetham Tower](/wiki/Beetham_Tower,_Manchester "Beetham Tower, Manchester") was the tallest UK building outside of [London](/wiki/London "London") and the highest residential accommodation in Europe when completed in 2006. It was surpassed in 2018 by the 659-foot (201 m) South Tower of the [Deansgate Square](/wiki/Deansgate_Square "Deansgate Square") project, also in Manchester.[[83]](#cite_note-Beetham_Tower-87)

On 22 May 2017, an [Islamist terrorist](/wiki/Islamist_terrorist "Islamist terrorist") carried out [a suicide bombing](/wiki/Manchester_Arena_bombing "Manchester Arena bombing") outside the [Manchester Arena](/wiki/Manchester_Arena "Manchester Arena"), shortly after an [Ariana Grande](/wiki/Ariana_Grande "Ariana Grande") concert.[[84]](#cite_note-88) The explosion killed 23 people (including the perpetrator) and injured over 800.[[85]](#cite_note-89) It was the deadliest terrorist attack and first suicide bombing in Britain since the [7 July 2005 London bombings](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_bombings "7 July 2005 London bombings") and changed the [UK's threat level](/wiki/UK_Threat_Levels "UK Threat Levels") to "critical" for the first time since 2007.[[86]](#cite_note-90) On 2 October 2025, [another terrorist attack](/wiki/Manchester_synagogue_attack "Manchester synagogue attack") occurred outside the [Heaton Park Hebrew Congregation](/wiki/Heaton_Park_Hebrew_Congregation "Heaton Park Hebrew Congregation") synagogue[[87]](#cite_note-91) when a 35-year-old drove a car into pedestrians, then stabbed worshippers.[[88]](#cite_note-92)

## Government

Main articles: [Politics in Manchester](/wiki/Politics_in_Manchester "Politics in Manchester") and [Manchester City Council](/wiki/Manchester_City_Council "Manchester City Council")

See also: [Manchester local elections](/wiki/Manchester_local_elections "Manchester local elections"), [List of Lord Mayors of Manchester](/wiki/List_of_Lord_Mayors_of_Manchester "List of Lord Mayors of Manchester"), and [Healthcare in Greater Manchester](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Greater_Manchester "Healthcare in Greater Manchester")

[![A grand gothic revival building as viewed from afar, with a gothic revival clock tower stretching high into the sky. Trees partially obstruct the view and the sky is clear with some clouds.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Manchester_Town_Hall_from_Lloyd_St.jpg/250px-Manchester_Town_Hall_from_Lloyd_St.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manchester_Town_Hall_from_Lloyd_St.jpg)

[Manchester Town Hall](/wiki/Manchester_Town_Hall "Manchester Town Hall") in [Albert Square](/wiki/Albert_Square,_Manchester "Albert Square, Manchester"), the seat of local government, is an example of [Victorian-era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") [Gothic revival](/wiki/Gothic_revival "Gothic revival") architecture.

The City of Manchester is governed by [Manchester City Council](/wiki/Manchester_City_Council "Manchester City Council"). The [Greater Manchester Combined Authority](/wiki/Greater_Manchester_Combined_Authority "Greater Manchester Combined Authority"), with a [directly elected mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_Greater_Manchester "Mayor of Greater Manchester"), has responsibilities for several areas such as economic strategy and transport on a Greater Manchester-wide basis. Manchester has been a member of the English [Core Cities Group](/wiki/Core_Cities_Group "Core Cities Group") since its inception in 1995.[[89]](#cite_note-Core_city-93)

The town of Manchester was granted a charter by Thomas Grelley in 1301 but lost its [borough status](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom") in a court case of 1359. Until the 19th century, local government was largely controlled by [manorial courts](/wiki/Manorial_court "Manorial court"), the last of which was dissolved in 1846. In 1792, Police Commissioners were established for the social improvement of Manchester. Manchester regained its borough status in 1838 and comprised the townships of [Beswick](/wiki/Beswick,_Greater_Manchester "Beswick, Greater Manchester"), [Cheetham Hill](/wiki/Cheetham_Hill "Cheetham Hill"), [Chorlton upon Medlock](/wiki/Chorlton_upon_Medlock "Chorlton upon Medlock") and [Hulme](/wiki/Hulme "Hulme"). By 1846, with increasing population and greater industrialisation, the Borough Council had assumed the powers of the Police Commissioners. In 1853, Manchester was granted [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"). Its boundaries have since been extended subsuming [Harpurhey](/wiki/Harpurhey "Harpurhey"), [Rusholme](/wiki/Rusholme "Rusholme") and parts of [Moss Side](/wiki/Moss_Side "Moss Side") and [Withington](/wiki/Withington "Withington") in 1885; [Burnage](/wiki/Burnage "Burnage"), [Chorlton-cum-Hardy](/wiki/Chorlton-cum-Hardy "Chorlton-cum-Hardy"), [Didsbury](/wiki/Didsbury "Didsbury"), [Fallowfield](/wiki/Fallowfield "Fallowfield"), [Levenshulme](/wiki/Levenshulme "Levenshulme"), [Longsight](/wiki/Longsight "Longsight"), [Withington](/wiki/Withington "Withington"), [Baguley](/wiki/Baguley "Baguley") and [Northenden](/wiki/Northenden "Northenden") between the 1880s and the 1930s;[[60]](#cite_note-GM_Gazetteer-64) and [Ringway](/wiki/Ringway,_Greater_Manchester "Ringway, Greater Manchester") in 1974.[[90]](#cite_note-94)

[![A fairly young man slightly smiling at the camera against green shrubbery as a background. He is wearing square glasses, a blue tie, and a grey blazer on a white shirt.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Andy_Burnham_2023.JPG/250px-Andy_Burnham_2023.JPG)](/wiki/File:Andy_Burnham_2023.JPG)

[Andy Burnham](/wiki/Andy_Burnham "Andy Burnham"), the [Mayor of Greater Manchester](/wiki/Mayor_of_Greater_Manchester "Mayor of Greater Manchester"), in 2023

In 2014, it was announced that Greater Manchester would have a directly elected mayor with fiscal control over health, transport, housing and police in ten local authorities which form the [Greater Manchester Combined Authority](/wiki/Greater_Manchester_Combined_Authority "Greater Manchester Combined Authority").[[91]](#cite_note-95) [Andy Burnham](/wiki/Andy_Burnham "Andy Burnham") was elected as the first [Mayor of Greater Manchester](/wiki/Mayor_of_Greater_Manchester "Mayor of Greater Manchester") in the [2017 election](/wiki/2017_Greater_Manchester_mayoral_election "2017 Greater Manchester mayoral election"),[[92]](#cite_note-96) and was re-elected in [2021](/wiki/2021_Greater_Manchester_mayoral_election "2021 Greater Manchester mayoral election")[[93]](#cite_note-97) and [2024](/wiki/2017_Greater_Manchester_mayoral_election "2017 Greater Manchester mayoral election").[[94]](#cite_note-:12-98) The mayor of Greater Manchester oversees a budget of £2.6bn in 2024, including £1.51bn is spent on policing and transport,[[95]](#cite_note-:13-99) making this the most powerful mayoral role in the country.[[94]](#cite_note-:12-98) Burnham is the [police and crime commissioner](/wiki/Police_and_crime_commissioner "Police and crime commissioner") for Greater Manchester [*ex officio*](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member") and is responsible for some housing, education, and welfare policies.[[95]](#cite_note-:13-99)

## Geography

See also: [Geography of Greater Manchester](/wiki/Geography_of_Greater_Manchester "Geography of Greater Manchester")

[![A map showing the boundary of the City of Manchester with the City Centre, Salford, and the airport also labelled. Almost all of the map, both inside and outside of the boundary, is shaded in grey, which denotes urban sprawl.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Map_of_Manchester.png/250px-Map_of_Manchester.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Manchester.png)

The City of Manchester. The [land use](/wiki/Land_use "Land use") is overwhelmingly urban.

At [53°28′0″N 2°14′0″W﻿ / ﻿53.46667°N 2.23333°W﻿ / 53.46667; -2.23333](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Manchester&params=53_28_0_N_2_14_0_W_type:city), 160 miles (260 km) northwest of London, Manchester lies in a bowl-shaped land area bordered to the north and east by the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines"), an upland chain that runs the length of [northern England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), and to the south by the [Cheshire Plain](/wiki/Cheshire_Plain "Cheshire Plain"). Manchester is 35.0 miles (56.3 km) north-east of [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool") and 35.0 miles (56.3 km) north-west of [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"), making the city the halfway point between the two. The [city centre](/wiki/Manchester_city_centre "Manchester city centre") is on the east bank of the [River Irwell](/wiki/River_Irwell "River Irwell"), near its confluences with the Rivers [Medlock](/wiki/River_Medlock "River Medlock") and [Irk](/wiki/River_Irk "River Irk"), and is relatively low-lying, being between 35 and 42 metres (115 and 138 feet) above sea level.[[96]](#cite_note-Topography-100)

The [River Mersey](/wiki/River_Mersey "River Mersey") flows through the south of Manchester. Much of the inner city, especially in the south, is flat, offering views from many highrise buildings in the city of the foothills and moors of the Pennines, which can often be capped with snow in the winter. Manchester's climate, its proximity to a [seaport](/wiki/Port "Port") at [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), the availability of waterpower from its rivers, and its nearby coal reserves were highly influential in its early development as an industrial city.[[97]](#cite_note-Coalfields-101)

For purposes of the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics"), Manchester forms the most populous settlement within the [Greater Manchester Urban Area](/wiki/Greater_Manchester_Urban_Area "Greater Manchester Urban Area"), the United Kingdom's second-largest conurbation. There is a mix of high-density urban and suburban locations. The largest open space in the city, at around 260 hectares (642 acres),[[98]](#cite_note-102) is [Heaton Park](/wiki/Heaton_Park "Heaton Park"). Manchester is contiguous on all sides with several large settlements, except for a small section along its southern boundary with [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire"). The [M60](/wiki/M60_motorway_(Great_Britain) "M60 motorway (Great Britain)") and [M56 motorways](/wiki/M56_motorway "M56 motorway") pass through [Northenden](/wiki/Northenden "Northenden") and [Wythenshawe](/wiki/Wythenshawe "Wythenshawe") respectively in the south of Manchester. Heavy rail lines enter the city from all directions, the principal destination being [Manchester Piccadilly station](/wiki/Manchester_Piccadilly_station "Manchester Piccadilly station"), the city's largest railway terminus, and the [second-busiest in Great Britain](/wiki/List_of_busiest_railway_stations_in_Great_Britain "List of busiest railway stations in Great Britain") outside of London.[[99]](#cite_note-103)

Manchester lies at the centre of the [North West Green Belt](/wiki/North_West_Green_Belt "North West Green Belt"). This reduces [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl"), prevents towns in the conurbation from further convergence, protects the identity of outlying communities, and preserves nearby countryside. It is achieved by restricting inappropriate development within the designated areas and imposing stricter conditions on permitted building.[[100]](#cite_note-belt2-104) Being highly urban, the borough contains limited protected [greenfield land](/wiki/Greenfield_land "Greenfield land") with minimal development opportunities.[[101]](#cite_note-105)

Manchester has a temperate [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*), like much of the British Isles, with warm summers and cold winters compared to other parts of the UK. Summer daytime temperatures regularly top 20 °C (68 °F). In recent years, temperatures have occasionally surpassed 30 °C (86 °F). There is regular but generally light precipitation throughout the year. The city's average annual rainfall is 806.6 mm (31.76 inches)[[102]](#cite_note-Manchester_weather-106) compared to a UK average of 1,125.0 mm (44.29 inches).[[103]](#cite_note-UK_weather-107) Its mean rain days are 140.4 per annum,[[102]](#cite_note-Manchester_weather-106) compared to the UK average of 154.4.[[103]](#cite_note-UK_weather-107)

Manchester has a relatively high humidity level; this, along with abundant soft water, was one factor that led to the advancement of the textile industry in the area.[[104]](#cite_note-108) Snowfalls are not common in the city because of the [urban warming](/wiki/Urban_climate "Urban climate") effect. The [West Pennine Moors](/wiki/West_Pennine_Moors "West Pennine Moors") to the north-west, [South Pennines](/wiki/South_Pennines "South Pennines") to the north-east and [Peak District](/wiki/Peak_District "Peak District") to the east receive more snow, which can close roads leading out of the city.[[105]](#cite_note-Snow-109) They include the [A62](/wiki/A62_road "A62 road") via [Oldham](/wiki/Oldham "Oldham") and [Standedge](/wiki/Standedge "Standedge"),[[106]](#cite_note-110) the [A57](/wiki/A57_road "A57 road"), [Snake Pass](/wiki/Snake_Pass "Snake Pass"), towards [Sheffield](/wiki/Sheffield "Sheffield"),[[107]](#cite_note-Peaks-111) and the [Pennine section of the M62](/wiki/M62_motorway#Milnrow_to_Outlane "M62 motorway").[[108]](#cite_note-112) The lowest temperature ever recorded in Manchester was −17.6 °C (0.3 °F) on 7 January 2010.[[109]](#cite_note-113) The highest was 38.0 °C (100.4 °F) on 19 July 2022, during the [2022 European Heatwave](/wiki/2022_European_heatwaves#July_heatwave_7 "2022 European heatwaves").[[110]](#cite_note-114)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Manchester](/wiki/Demographics_of_Manchester "Demographics of Manchester")

[![A population pyramid that is fairly symmetrical and slowly widens as it goes down before suddenly dropping off and becoming fairly regular again.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Manchester_population_pyramid.svg/250px-Manchester_population_pyramid.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Manchester_population_pyramid.svg)

City of Manchester population pyramid in 2021

[![A population pyramid that is fairly symmetrical and widens as it goes down except a dent in the middle, before tapering off and staying fairly even near the bottom.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/UK_and_foreign_born_population_pyramid_of_Manchester_in_2021.svg/250px-UK_and_foreign_born_population_pyramid_of_Manchester_in_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:UK_and_foreign_born_population_pyramid_of_Manchester_in_2021.svg)

The UK- and foreign-born population pyramid of Manchester in 2021

In the [2021 United Kingdom census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), the population of the City of Manchester was 552,000, an increase of 9.7% from the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_United_Kingdom_census "2011 United Kingdom census").[[118]](#cite_note-122) It was slower than the increase between 2001 and 2021 of 20.8%, which was the largest in the UK outside of London.[[119]](#cite_note-123) The growth was higher than the forecasted rate of growth of 5.8%.[[120]](#cite_note-mancpop-124) 43.5% of people had never [married](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage"), 37% were married, 12.24% were [separated](/wiki/Marital_separation "Marital separation") or [divorced](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"), and 7.26% were [widowed](/wiki/Widow "Widow").[[121]](#cite_note-125)

Since 1991, the City of Manchester has grown by 36.3%, faster than other major cities in England. In 2012, 6,547,000 people lived within 30 miles (50 km) of Manchester and 11,694,000 within 50 miles (80 km).[[120]](#cite_note-mancpop-124) In 2011/2012, births exceeded deaths by 4,800.[[120]](#cite_note-mancpop-124) Manchester has a younger population than the average for England: nationally, 82.6% of people are below the age of 65 compared to 91.2% for the City of Manchester. Greater Manchester Combined Authority's analysis of the 2021 census noticed the rising number of 0–15 year olds was a large drive for the increasing population.[[122]](#cite_note-:3-126) The Manchester [Larger Urban Zone](/wiki/Larger_Urban_Zone "Larger Urban Zone"), a [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat") measure of the functional city-region approximated to local government districts, had a population of 2,539,100 in 2004.[[123]](#cite_note-urbanaudit-127)[[e]](#cite_note-129)

### Religion

Religious beliefs, 2021 census.[[125]](#cite_note-130)

1. Christian (36.2%)
2. No Religion (32.4%)
3. Muslim (22.3%)
4. Hindu (1.10%)
5. Buddhist (0.60%)
6. Jewish (0.50%)
7. Other (0.50%)
8. Religion Not Stated (5.90%)

Since the 2001 census, the proportion of Christians in Manchester has fallen from 62.4% to 48.7% in 2011. The proportion of those with no religious affiliation rose from 16% to 25.3%. The proportion of Muslims increased from 9.1% to 15.8%. The size of the Jewish population in Greater Manchester is the largest in Britain outside London.[[126]](#cite_note-Jewish_Population-131)

### Ethnicity

[![A Chinese arch structure on a street with a brick building to the right and no building to the left. There is a tree in the background and it is a clear early evening sky.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Manchester_Chinatown_2023.jpg/250px-Manchester_Chinatown_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manchester_Chinatown_2023.jpg)

[Manchester Chinatown](/wiki/Manchester_Chinatown "Manchester Chinatown")'s Paifang arch

[![An animated gif of a series of pie charts between 1971 and 2021, which show the proportion of White people decreasing from 95.8% to 48.7% and various other demographics appearing.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Manchester_ethnic_demography_over_time.gif/250px-Manchester_ethnic_demography_over_time.gif)](/wiki/File:Manchester_ethnic_demography_over_time.gif)

The ethnic demography of Manchester from 1971 to 2021

The City of Manchester has the highest non-white proportion of any district in Greater Manchester. The [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census") showed that 56.8% of the population was [White](/wiki/White_people "White people"). 48.7% were [White British](/wiki/White_British "White British"), 1.7% [White Irish](/wiki/Irish_migration_to_Great_Britain "Irish migration to Great Britain"), 0.1% [Gypsy](/wiki/Gypsy_(term) "Gypsy (term)") or [Irish Traveller](/wiki/Irish_Traveller "Irish Traveller"), and 6.2% [Other White](/wiki/Other_White "Other White"). The size of mixed European and British ethnic groups is unclear.[[127]](#cite_note-132) 5.2% were [mixed race](/wiki/Mixed_(United_Kingdom_ethnicity_category) "Mixed (United Kingdom ethnicity category)") (1.8% White and Black Caribbean, 1.1% White and Black African, 1.1% White and Asian, 1.2% other mixed), 20.9% [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") (2.7% [Indian](/wiki/British_Indian "British Indian"), 11.9% [Pakistani](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis"), 1.8% [Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi"), 2.3% [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese"), 2.2% other Asian), 12% [Black](/wiki/Black_British "Black British") (8.7% African, 1.9% [Caribbean](/wiki/British_African-Caribbean_people "British African-Caribbean people"), 1.4% [other Black](/wiki/Other_Black "Other Black")), 2.7% [Arab](/wiki/British_Arab "British Arab") and 2.4% of other ethnic heritage.[[128]](#cite_note-:2-133)

[Moss Side](/wiki/Moss_Side "Moss Side"), [Longsight](/wiki/Longsight "Longsight"), [Cheetham Hill](/wiki/Cheetham_Hill "Cheetham Hill"), and [Rusholme](/wiki/Rusholme "Rusholme") are population centres for ethnic minorities.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39) Manchester's Irish Festival, including a [St Patrick's Day](/wiki/St_Patrick%27s_Day "St Patrick's Day") parade, is one of Europe's largest.[[129]](#cite_note-Irish_festival-134) There is a well-established [Chinatown in the city](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manchester "Chinatown, Manchester"), which attracts large numbers of Chinese university students[[130]](#cite_note-Chinatown-135) who contribute to Manchester having the third-largest Chinese population in Europe.[[131]](#cite_note-136)[[132]](#cite_note-137)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Manchester](/wiki/Economy_of_Manchester "Economy of Manchester")

See also: [List of companies based in Greater Manchester](/wiki/List_of_companies_based_in_Greater_Manchester "List of companies based in Greater Manchester")

### Macroeconomic wealth

*City of Manchester GVA (Balanced)  
2013–2023*
