The [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") does not produce economic data for the City of Manchester alone; instead it groups the city with [Salford](/wiki/City_of_Salford "City of Salford"), [Stockport](/wiki/Stockport "Stockport"), [Tameside](/wiki/Tameside "Tameside"), and [Trafford](/wiki/Trafford "Trafford") in an area named Greater Manchester South.[[141]](#cite_note-Leeds.gov.uk-146) In 2023, the area had a [GVA](/wiki/Gross_Value_Added "Gross Value Added") of £34.8 billion. The economy grew relatively strongly between 2002 and 2012, when growth was 2.3% above the national average.[[141]](#cite_note-Leeds.gov.uk-146)

It is ranked as a Beta– ([beta minus](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Beta_− "Globalization and World Cities Research Network")) city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") in their 2024 rankings, placing it second for UK cities behind London, which is A++ (the highest ranking).[[142]](#cite_note-147) As the UK economy continues to recover from its 2008–2010 downturn, Manchester compares favourably according to recent figures. In 2012 it showed the strongest annual growth in business stock (5%) of all [core cities](/wiki/Core_Cities_Group "Core Cities Group").[[143]](#cite_note-Business_Demography:_Enterprise_Births,_Deaths_and_Survival_Rates_for_2011-148)

The decade between 2015 and 2025 saw the economy significantly affected by the country's withdrawal from the European Union ([Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit")) and by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_Kingdom "COVID-19 pandemic in the United Kingdom").[[144]](#cite_note-149)[[145]](#cite_note-150) Estimates showed a decline in economic output from COVID in the region of 9–10%, and with only 1% of firms in the city reporting a positive impact from Brexit, with 60% reporting a neutral or negative impact.[[146]](#cite_note-151) The years since 2021 have seen some recovery, with forecasts for 2025–28 suggesting that growth in the region will reach 2.4% annually, exceeding the expected national growth rate of 1.6%.[[147]](#cite_note-152)

### Individual wealth

Some of the country's most deprived and most affluent neighbourhoods are found in Manchester.[[148]](#cite_note-153)[[149]](#cite_note-154) As of the 2019 [Indices of Multiple Deprivation](/wiki/Indices_of_Multiple_Deprivation "Indices of Multiple Deprivation"), Manchester is the second most deprived local authority by rank, the sixth by score, and fifth by the proportion of [Lower Layer Super Output Areas](/wiki/Lower_Layer_Super_Output_Area "Lower Layer Super Output Area") (LLSOAs) that are deprived, with 43% of its LLOAs falling among the top 10% of areas nationally by the extent of deprivation. By final ranking it is only beaten by [Blackpool](/wiki/Blackpool "Blackpool").[[150]](#cite_note-155) As of the 2021 census, 53.5% of the over-16 population is employed, 5.7% are unemployed while actively seeking work, and 40.8% are economically inactive.[[151]](#cite_note-156)

Greater Manchester is home to more multi-millionaires than anywhere outside London, with the City of Manchester taking up most of the tally.[[152]](#cite_note-157) In 2013 Manchester was ranked 6th in the UK for quality of life, according to a rating of the UK's 12 largest cities.[[153]](#cite_note-qol-158) Women fare better in Manchester than the rest of the country in comparative pay with men. The per hours-worked [gender pay gap](/wiki/Gender_pay_gap "Gender pay gap") is 3.3% compared with 11.1% for Britain.[[154]](#cite_note-2013_labour_market_profile-159) 37% of the working-age population in Manchester have degree-level qualifications, as opposed to an average of 33% across other core cities,[[154]](#cite_note-2013_labour_market_profile-159) although its schools under-perform slightly compared with the national average.[[155]](#cite_note-160)

### Business wealth

[![An aerial image of a city at night. There are five skyscrapers in the foreground and the photo is misty with tall hills and low-lying clouds in the background. The lights of the rest of the city can be seen in the background but not the buildings.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Great_Jackson_Street_Framework_2020.jpg/250px-Great_Jackson_Street_Framework_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Jackson_Street_Framework_2020.jpg)

The Great Jackson Street skyscraper district under construction in Central Manchester

Manchester owns two of the country's four busiest airports and uses its earnings to fund local projects.[[156]](#cite_note-161) [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG")'s competitive alternative report found that in 2012 Manchester had the 9th lowest tax cost of any industrialised city in the world,[[157]](#cite_note-indices-162) and fiscal devolution has come earlier to Manchester than to any other British city: it can keep half the extra taxes it gets from transport investment.[[158]](#cite_note-econ-163) KPMG's competitive alternative report also found that Manchester was Europe's most affordable city featured, ranking slightly better than the Dutch cities of [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam") and [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), which all have a cost-of-living index of less than 95.[[157]](#cite_note-indices-162)

Manchester has the largest UK office market outside London, according to GVA Grimley, with a quarterly office uptake (averaged over 2010–2014) of some 250,000 square feet (23,000 m2) – 90,000 square feet (8,400 m2) more than the nearest rival, Birmingham.[[159]](#cite_note-164) The strong office market in Manchester has been partly attributed to "northshoring" (from [offshoring](/wiki/Offshoring "Offshoring")), which entails the relocation or alternative creation of jobs away from the South to areas where office space is possibly cheaper and the workforce market less saturated.[[160]](#cite_note-165)

## Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Manchester](/wiki/Architecture_of_Manchester "Architecture of Manchester")

See also: [List of tallest buildings and structures in Manchester](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_and_structures_in_Manchester "List of tallest buildings and structures in Manchester"), [List of streets and roads in Manchester](/wiki/List_of_streets_and_roads_in_Manchester "List of streets and roads in Manchester"), [Grade I listed buildings in Greater Manchester](/wiki/Grade_I_listed_buildings_in_Greater_Manchester "Grade I listed buildings in Greater Manchester"), [Grade II\* listed buildings in Greater Manchester](/wiki/Grade_II*_listed_buildings_in_Greater_Manchester "Grade II* listed buildings in Greater Manchester"), and [List of public art in Greater Manchester](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Greater_Manchester "List of public art in Greater Manchester")

[![A red brick and terracotta building that sits on a corner of a square junction. The corner of the building is slightly rounded into an octagon-like shape with it then stretching above the building to become a clock tower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/67_Whitworth_Street.jpg/250px-67_Whitworth_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:67_Whitworth_Street.jpg)

Neo-baroque [Lancaster House](/wiki/Lancaster_House,_Manchester "Lancaster House, Manchester"). Manchester is known for opulent warehouses from the city's textile trade.

Manchester's buildings display a variety of architectural styles, ranging from [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") to [contemporary architecture](/wiki/Contemporary_architecture "Contemporary architecture"). The widespread use of [red brick](/wiki/Red_brick "Red brick") characterises the city; much of the architecture of which harks back to its days as a global centre for the cotton trade.[[40]](#cite_note-Hartwell-44) Just outside the immediate city centre are a large number of former [cotton mills](/wiki/Cotton_mill "Cotton mill"), some of which have been left virtually untouched since their closure, while many have been redeveloped as apartment buildings and office space. [Manchester Town Hall](/wiki/Manchester_Town_Hall "Manchester Town Hall"), in [Albert Square](/wiki/Albert_Square,_Manchester "Albert Square, Manchester"), was built in the [Gothic revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") style.[[161]](#cite_note-166)

Manchester has several [skyscrapers](/wiki/Tallest_Buildings_of_Manchester "Tallest Buildings of Manchester") built in the 1960s and 1970s; the tallest was the [CIS Tower](/wiki/CIS_Tower "CIS Tower") near [Manchester Victoria station](/wiki/Manchester_Victoria_station "Manchester Victoria station") until the [Beetham Tower](/wiki/Beetham_Tower,_Manchester "Beetham Tower, Manchester") was completed in 2006. The latter exemplifies a new surge in high-rise building. It includes a [Hilton hotel](/wiki/Hilton_Hotels "Hilton Hotels"), a restaurant and apartments. The largest skyscraper is now Deansgate Square South Tower, at 201 m (659 ft). [The Green Building](/wiki/The_Green_Building "The Green Building"), opposite [Oxford Road station](/wiki/Manchester_Oxford_Road_railway_station "Manchester Oxford Road railway station"), is an eco-friendly housing project, while the recently completed [One Angel Square](/wiki/One_Angel_Square "One Angel Square") is one of the most sustainable large buildings in the world.[[162]](#cite_note-167)

### Landmarks

Albert Square and St Peter's Square hold many of Manchester's public monuments. Albert Square has monuments to [Prince Albert](/wiki/Prince_Albert_of_Saxe-Coburg-Gotha "Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha"), [Bishop James Fraser](/wiki/James_Fraser_(bishop) "James Fraser (bishop)"), [Oliver Heywood](/wiki/Oliver_Heywood "Oliver Heywood"), [William Gladstone](/wiki/William_Ewart_Gladstone "William Ewart Gladstone") and [John Bright](/wiki/John_Bright "John Bright"). [Piccadilly Gardens](/wiki/Piccadilly_Gardens "Piccadilly Gardens") has monuments dedicated to [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom"), [Robert Peel](/wiki/Robert_Peel "Robert Peel"), [James Watt](/wiki/James_Watt "James Watt") and the [Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington"). [The cenotaph](/wiki/Manchester_Cenotaph "Manchester Cenotaph") in St Peter's Square is Manchester's main memorial to its war dead. Designed by [Edwin Lutyens](/wiki/Edwin_Lutyens "Edwin Lutyens"), it echoes [the original on Whitehall](/wiki/The_Cenotaph,_Whitehall "The Cenotaph, Whitehall") in London. The [Alan Turing Memorial](/wiki/Alan_Turing_Memorial "Alan Turing Memorial") in [Sackville Park](/wiki/Sackville_Park "Sackville Park") commemorates his role as the father of modern computing. A larger-than-life statue of [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") by George Gray Barnard in the eponymous Lincoln Square (having stood for many years in [Platt Fields](/wiki/Platt_Fields "Platt Fields")) was presented to the city by [Charles Phelps Taft](/wiki/Charles_Phelps_Taft "Charles Phelps Taft") and Anna Sinton to mark the part Lancashire played in the [cotton famine](/wiki/Cotton_famine "Cotton famine") and [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") of 1861–1865.[[163]](#cite_note-PSGM-168)

Adjacent to [Manchester Airport](/wiki/Manchester_Airport "Manchester Airport") is the Runway Visitor Park, an aviation centre which is the site of [G-BOAC](/wiki/G-BOAC "G-BOAC"),[[164]](#cite_note-169) one of the [twenty Concorde aircraft](/wiki/List_of_Concorde_aircraft "List of Concorde aircraft") built.[[165]](#cite_note-towey-2007-170) The aircraft was the flagship of [British Airways](/wiki/British_Airways "British Airways")'s fleet because BOAC was the initials of the [British Overseas Airways Corporation](/wiki/British_Overseas_Airways_Corporation "British Overseas Airways Corporation").[[166]](#cite_note-171) Other aircraft on display at the park are a [BAE Systems Nimrod MRA4](/wiki/BAE_Systems_Nimrod_MRA4 "BAE Systems Nimrod MRA4"), a [Hawker Siddeley Trident](/wiki/Hawker_Siddeley_Trident "Hawker Siddeley Trident"), a [McDonnell Douglas DC-10](/wiki/McDonnell_Douglas_DC-10 "McDonnell Douglas DC-10"), and a [British Aerospace 146](/wiki/British_Aerospace_146 "British Aerospace 146").[[167]](#cite_note-172)

[Heaton Park](/wiki/Heaton_Park "Heaton Park") in the north of the city borough is one of the largest municipal parks in Europe, covering 610 acres (250 ha) of parkland.[[168]](#cite_note-HeatonPark-173) The city has 135 parks, gardens, and open spaces.[[169]](#cite_note-Parks-174) Manchester has six designated [local nature reserves](/wiki/Local_nature_reserve "Local nature reserve"): [Chorlton Water Park](/wiki/Chorlton_Water_Park "Chorlton Water Park"), Blackley Forest, Clayton Vale and Chorlton Ees, Ivy Green, [Boggart Hole Clough](/wiki/Boggart_Hole_Clough "Boggart Hole Clough") and [Highfield Country Park](/wiki/Highfield_Country_Park "Highfield Country Park").[[170]](#cite_note-175)

[![A skyline of a city against a blue sky and tall hills in the background. There are around nine skyscrapers distributed fairly evenly across the skyline with many cranes also visible. In the foreground there is a river with trees on both banks.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Manchester_City_Centre_Skyline.jpg/960px-Manchester_City_Centre_Skyline.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manchester_City_Centre_Skyline.jpg "The city skyline as photographed in January 2020")

The city skyline as photographed in January 2020

## Transport

Main article: [Transport in Manchester](/wiki/Transport_in_Manchester "Transport in Manchester")

See also: [Transport for Greater Manchester](/wiki/Transport_for_Greater_Manchester "Transport for Greater Manchester")

### Rail

[Manchester Liverpool Road](/wiki/Manchester_Liverpool_Road_railway_station "Manchester Liverpool Road railway station") was the world's first purpose-built passenger and goods railway station[[171]](#cite_note-176) and served as the Manchester terminus on the [Liverpool and Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool and Manchester Railway"), the first [inter-city](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") passenger railway in the world. The station [opened with the railway](/wiki/Opening_of_the_Liverpool_and_Manchester_Railway "Opening of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway") in 1830 and closed in 1975. Since 1983 they have been part of the site of the [Science and Industry Museum](/wiki/Science_and_Industry_Museum "Science and Industry Museum").[[172]](#cite_note-177)

[![An aerial photo of a modern railway terminus station viewed from the top end a short distance away. The multiple parallel roofs of the trainshed stretch into the background against a grey sky.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Piccadilly_Station_Manchester_-_geograph.org.uk_-_692981.jpg/250px-Piccadilly_Station_Manchester_-_geograph.org.uk_-_692981.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piccadilly_Station_Manchester_-_geograph.org.uk_-_692981.jpg)

[Manchester Piccadilly railway station](/wiki/Manchester_Piccadilly_station "Manchester Piccadilly station"), the busiest of the four major railway stations in the [Manchester station group](/wiki/Manchester_station_group "Manchester station group"), with over 32 million passengers using the station in 2019/20[[173]](#cite_note-ORR-178)

Two of the city's four main line terminus stations, [Manchester Central](/wiki/Manchester_Central_railway_station "Manchester Central railway station") and [Manchester Exchange](/wiki/Manchester_Exchange_railway_station "Manchester Exchange railway station"), closed to passengers in 1969.[[174]](#cite_note-179) [Manchester Mayfield station](/wiki/Manchester_Mayfield_railway_station "Manchester Mayfield railway station") closed to passenger services in 1960[[175]](#cite_note-Clinker-180) and to freight in 1986.[[176]](#cite_note-subbrit2-181) In 2025 Manchester City Council approved the redevelopment of the Mayfield site into a [housing estate](/wiki/Housing_estate "Housing estate").[[177]](#cite_note-182)

The [Northern Hub](/wiki/Northern_Hub "Northern Hub") improvement programme in the 2010s built electrification schemes into and through Manchester, organised redevelopment of Victoria station and led construction of the [Ordsall Chord](/wiki/Ordsall_Chord "Ordsall Chord") directly linking Victoria and Piccadilly.[[178]](#cite_note-183) The city is served by its local rail network that is now working to capacity.[[179]](#cite_note-184) It is also the centre of a county-wide railway network, including the [West Coast Main Line](/wiki/West_Coast_Main_Line "West Coast Main Line"), with two mainline stations: [Manchester Piccadilly](/wiki/Manchester_Piccadilly_railway_station "Manchester Piccadilly railway station") and [Manchester Victoria](/wiki/Manchester_Victoria_railway_station "Manchester Victoria railway station"). The [Manchester station group](/wiki/Manchester_station_group "Manchester station group") – comprising Manchester Piccadilly, Manchester Victoria, [Manchester Oxford Road](/wiki/Manchester_Oxford_Road_railway_station "Manchester Oxford Road railway station") and [Deansgate](/wiki/Deansgate_railway_station "Deansgate railway station") – is the third busiest in the UK, with 44.9 million passengers recorded in 2017/2018.[[173]](#cite_note-ORR-178) The city centre, specifically the [Castlefield Corridor](/wiki/Castlefield_Corridor "Castlefield Corridor"), suffers from constrained rail capacity causing delays and cancellations – a 2018 report found that all three major Manchester stations are among the ten worst stations in the United Kingdom for punctuality, with Oxford Road deemed the worst in the country.[[180]](#cite_note-185)

[![Two yellow trams sit besides one another on parallel tracks, with the one on the left slightly closer to the camera which is on the ground in the road. Both have their pantographs up which are touching contact wires. On either side there are people walking on the pavement.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Two_M5000_trams_passing.jpg/250px-Two_M5000_trams_passing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_M5000_trams_passing.jpg)

Manchester Metrolink is the [largest tram system in the UK](/wiki/Transport_in_the_United_Kingdom#Trams_and_light_rail "Transport in the United Kingdom"), with a total route length of 64 miles (103 km).[[181]](#cite_note-186)

Manchester became the first city in the UK to acquire a modern [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") tram system when the [Manchester Metrolink](/wiki/Manchester_Metrolink "Manchester Metrolink") opened in 1992. In 2023–2024, 42 million passenger journeys were made on the system.[[182]](#cite_note-dft23-187) The system mostly runs on former commuter rail lines converted for light rail use, and crosses the city centre via on-street tram lines.[[183]](#cite_note-metrolink-history-188) The network consists of eight lines with [99 stops](/wiki/List_of_Manchester_Metrolink_tram_stops "List of Manchester Metrolink tram stops").[[184]](#cite_note-RTC-189) Manchester city centre is also serviced by over a dozen heavy and light rail-based park and ride sites.[[185]](#cite_note-Park_&_Ride-190)

### Bus

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/SM_81_13192.jpg/250px-SM_81_13192.jpg)](/wiki/File:SM_81_13192.jpg)

Buses at [Piccadilly Gardens bus station](/wiki/Piccadilly_Gardens_bus_station "Piccadilly Gardens bus station")

The city has one of the most extensive bus networks outside London. Before the rollout of [Bee Network](/wiki/Bee_Network "Bee Network") [bus franchising](/wiki/Bus_franchising "Bus franchising") across three tranches between 24 September 2023 and 5 January 2025, there were over 50 bus companies operating in the [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester") region radiating from the city. In 2011, 80% of public transport journeys in Greater Manchester were made by bus, amounting to 220 million passenger journeys each year.[[186]](#cite_note-2012_Annual_Report-191)

After [deregulation](/wiki/Bus_deregulation_in_the_United_Kingdom "Bus deregulation in the United Kingdom") in 1986, the bus system was taken over by [GM Buses](/wiki/GM_Buses "GM Buses"), which after privatisation was split into GM Buses North and GM Buses South; these were taken over by [First Greater Manchester](/wiki/First_Greater_Manchester "First Greater Manchester") and [Stagecoach Manchester](/wiki/Stagecoach_Manchester "Stagecoach Manchester") respectively. Much of the First Greater Manchester business was sold to [Diamond North West](/wiki/Diamond_North_West "Diamond North West") and [Go North West](/wiki/Go_North_West "Go North West") in 2019.[[187]](#cite_note-192) Stagecoach Manchester, one of the [Stagecoach Group](/wiki/Stagecoach_Group "Stagecoach Group")'s largest subsidiaries and formerly the largest bus operator in Greater Manchester,[[188]](#cite_note-193) operates a two-route [zero-fare](/wiki/Zero-fare_public_transport "Zero-fare public transport") [free bus network](/wiki/Free_buses_in_Greater_Manchester "Free buses in Greater Manchester"), which carries 2.8 million commuters a year[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] around Manchester's business districts.[[186]](#cite_note-2012_Annual_Report-191)[[189]](#cite_note-194)[[190]](#cite_note-195)

### Air

Main article: [Manchester Airport](/wiki/Manchester_Airport "Manchester Airport")

[![An aerial view of a large airport; from left to right there are two parallel runways and then a series of terminal buildings around a central flat area. In the foreground the airport backs onto urban sprawl but in the background it meets fields.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Manchester_Airport_EGCC.jpg/250px-Manchester_Airport_EGCC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manchester_Airport_EGCC.jpg)

Manchester Airport from above

Manchester Airport is the [third busiest in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Busiest_airports_in_the_United_Kingdom_by_total_passenger_traffic#2010_/_2011_data "Busiest airports in the United Kingdom by total passenger traffic"), with [over double the number of annual passengers](/wiki/Busiest_airports_in_the_United_Kingdom_by_total_passenger_traffic#2012_/_2013_data "Busiest airports in the United Kingdom by total passenger traffic") of the next busiest non-London airport.[[191]](#cite_note-caa1990-196) Services cover many international destinations, more than any other airport in Britain.[[192]](#cite_note-197) It is the only airport in the UK outside London to have two fully-operational runways.[[193]](#cite_note-198) It is an "*Category 10*" airport, able to handle "*Code F*" aircraft, including the [Airbus A380](/wiki/Airbus_A380 "Airbus A380"),[[194]](#cite_note-199) one of only 17 airports in the world and one of only three UK airports to operate the Airbus.[[195]](#cite_note-200)

A smaller [Manchester Barton Aerodrome](/wiki/Manchester_Barton_Aerodrome "Manchester Barton Aerodrome") exists 9.3 km (6 mi) to the west of Manchester city centre. It was Manchester's first municipal airport, site of the first [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control") tower in the UK, and the first municipal airfield to be licensed by the [Air Ministry](/wiki/Air_Ministry "Air Ministry").[[196]](#cite_note-CAMHIST-201) Private [charter flights](/wiki/Air_charter "Air charter") and [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") use City. It also has a [flight school](/wiki/Flight_training "Flight training"),[[197]](#cite_note-202) and both the [Greater Manchester Police Air Support Unit](/wiki/Greater_Manchester_Police#Air_Support_Unit "Greater Manchester Police") and the [North West Air Ambulance](/wiki/North_West_Air_Ambulance "North West Air Ambulance") have helicopters based there.[[198]](#cite_note-203)[[199]](#cite_note-204)

### Canal

An extensive canal network passes through Manchester including the [Ashton Canal](/wiki/Ashton_Canal "Ashton Canal"), [Rochdale Canal](/wiki/Rochdale_Canal "Rochdale Canal") and [Bridgewater Canal](/wiki/Bridgewater_Canal "Bridgewater Canal") – all of which end in Manchester city centre. The canals are still maintained, though now largely repurposed for leisure use. The [Manchester Ship Canal](/wiki/Manchester_Ship_Canal "Manchester Ship Canal"), which was built to carry freight from the Industrial Revolution onward, ends in neighbouring Salford before linking with the River Irwell which runs through the north of the city.[[200]](#cite_note-205)

### Cycling

Further information: [Cycling in Greater Manchester](/wiki/Cycling_in_Greater_Manchester "Cycling in Greater Manchester")

Cycling for transportation and leisure is popular in Manchester and the city plays a major role in British cycle racing.[[201]](#cite_note-206)[[202]](#cite_note-:15-207) As of 2023, 2% of journeys in Manchester are made by bicycle,[[203]](#cite_note-208) with cycle routes being integrated into Manchester's multimodal [Bee Network](/wiki/Bee_Network "Bee Network") alongside walking, train, tram, and bus routes.[[204]](#cite_note-209)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Manchester](/wiki/Culture_of_Manchester "Culture of Manchester")

See also: [List of people from Manchester](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Manchester "List of people from Manchester")

### Music

See also: [Popular music of Manchester](/wiki/Popular_music_of_Manchester "Popular music of Manchester"), [List of music artists and bands from Manchester](/wiki/List_of_music_artists_and_bands_from_Manchester "List of music artists and bands from Manchester"), and [Madchester](/wiki/Madchester "Madchester")

Manchester acts of the 1960s include [the Hollies](/wiki/The_Hollies "The Hollies"), [Herman's Hermits](/wiki/Herman%27s_Hermits "Herman's Hermits"), and [Davy Jones](/wiki/Davy_Jones_(musician) "Davy Jones (musician)") of the [Monkees](/wiki/Monkees "Monkees"), and the earlier [Bee Gees](/wiki/Bee_Gees "Bee Gees"), who grew up in [Chorlton](/wiki/Chorlton-cum-Hardy "Chorlton-cum-Hardy").[[205]](#cite_note-BeeGees-210) In the 1980s the city was credited as the main driving force behind British [indie](/wiki/Indie_music "Indie music") music led by [The Smiths](/wiki/The_Smiths "The Smiths"), later including [The Stone Roses](/wiki/The_Stone_Roses "The Stone Roses"), [Happy Mondays](/wiki/Happy_Mondays "Happy Mondays"), and [James](/wiki/James_(band) "James (band)"). The later groups came from the [Madchester](/wiki/Madchester "Madchester") scene that centred on [The Haçienda](/wiki/The_Ha%C3%A7ienda "The Haçienda") nightclub developed by the founder of [Factory Records](/wiki/Factory_Records "Factory Records"), [Tony Wilson](/wiki/Tony_Wilson "Tony Wilson"). The former Smiths frontman [Morrissey](/wiki/Morrissey "Morrissey"), whose lyrics often refer to Manchester, later found international success as a solo artist. [Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(band) "Oasis (band)") formed in Manchester in 1991.[[206]](#cite_note-211) [Rap](/wiki/UK_rap "UK rap") artists from Manchester include [Bugzy Malone](/wiki/Bugzy_Malone "Bugzy Malone"),[[207]](#cite_note-PL-212) [Aitch](/wiki/Aitch_(rapper) "Aitch (rapper)"),[[208]](#cite_note-occ2019-213) and [Meekz](/wiki/Meekz "Meekz").[[209]](#cite_note-214)

[Brass band](/wiki/British_brass_band "British brass band") music, a tradition in the north of England, is important to Manchester's musical heritage;[[210]](#cite_note-mif-deller-215) some of the UK's leading bands, such as the [CWS](/wiki/Co-operative_wholesale_society "Co-operative wholesale society") Manchester Band and the [Fairey Band](/wiki/Fairey_Band "Fairey Band"), are from Manchester and surrounding areas, and the [Whit Friday](/wiki/Whit_Friday "Whit Friday") brass-band contest takes place annually in the neighbouring areas of [Saddleworth](/wiki/Saddleworth "Saddleworth") and [Tameside](/wiki/Tameside "Tameside").[[211]](#cite_note-216)

[![A glass and steel building on the left of a photograph, as viewed from the bottom of a large set of outdoor stairs leading up to it. The view of the building is partially obscured by lamp posts and the stairs are split up by steel railings](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/MEN_Arena%2C_Manchester_%287263927380%29.jpg/250px-MEN_Arena%2C_Manchester_%287263927380%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:MEN_Arena,_Manchester_(7263927380).jpg)

The Manchester Arena, the city's premier indoor multi-use venue and one of the [largest purpose-built arenas](/wiki/List_of_indoor_arenas_in_Europe "List of indoor arenas in Europe") in Europe

Manchester's main pop music venue is [Manchester Arena](/wiki/Manchester_Arena "Manchester Arena"), voted "International Venue of the Year" in 2007.[[212]](#cite_note-MEN-217) With over 21,000 seats, it is the second largest arena of its type in Europe.[[212]](#cite_note-MEN-217) In terms of concertgoers, as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Manchester&action=edit) it is the busiest indoor arena in the world.[[213]](#cite_note-218) Other venues include [Manchester Apollo](/wiki/O2_Apollo_Manchester "O2 Apollo Manchester"), [Albert Hall](/wiki/Albert_Hall,_Manchester "Albert Hall, Manchester"), [Victoria Warehouse](/wiki/Victoria_Warehouse "Victoria Warehouse"), [Manchester Academy](/wiki/Manchester_Academy "Manchester Academy") and the [Co-op Live](/wiki/Co-op_Live "Co-op Live") arena, the latter being the largest indoor arena in the UK by capacity, and the [third largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_indoor_arenas_by_capacity "List of indoor arenas by capacity"). Smaller venues include the [Band on the Wall](/wiki/Band_on_the_Wall "Band on the Wall"), the Night and Day Café,[[214]](#cite_note-219) the Ruby Lounge,[[215]](#cite_note-220) The Deaf Institute,[[216]](#cite_note-221) and Gorilla. Manchester also has the most [indie](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") and rock music events outside London.[[217]](#cite_note-222)

Manchester has two [symphony orchestras](/wiki/Symphony_orchestra "Symphony orchestra"), [The Hallé](/wiki/The_Hall%C3%A9 "The Hallé") and the [BBC Philharmonic](/wiki/BBC_Philharmonic "BBC Philharmonic"), and a [chamber orchestra](/wiki/Chamber_orchestra "Chamber orchestra"), the Manchester Camerata. In the 1950s, the city was home to a "[Manchester School](/wiki/New_Music_Manchester "New Music Manchester")" of classical composers, which comprised [Harrison Birtwistle](/wiki/Harrison_Birtwistle "Harrison Birtwistle"), [Peter Maxwell Davies](/wiki/Peter_Maxwell_Davies "Peter Maxwell Davies"), [David Ellis](/wiki/David_Ellis_(composer) "David Ellis (composer)") and [Alexander Goehr](/wiki/Alexander_Goehr "Alexander Goehr"). Manchester is a centre for musical education with the [Royal Northern College of Music](/wiki/Royal_Northern_College_of_Music "Royal Northern College of Music") and [Chetham's School of Music](/wiki/Chetham%27s_School_of_Music "Chetham's School of Music").[[218]](#cite_note-223) Forerunners of the RNCM were the [Northern School of Music](/wiki/Northern_School_of_Music "Northern School of Music") (founded 1920) and the [Royal Manchester College of Music](/wiki/Royal_Manchester_College_of_Music "Royal Manchester College of Music") (founded 1893), which merged in 1973. The main classical music venue was the [Free Trade Hall](/wiki/Free_Trade_Hall "Free Trade Hall") on Peter Street until the opening in 1996 of the 2,500 seat [Bridgewater Hall](/wiki/Bridgewater_Hall "Bridgewater Hall").[[219]](#cite_note-224)

### Performing arts

[![A palladian square building as viewed from its corner. Outside the front of the building is a large pedestrianised square which is quite busy with people. The back right corner of the building continues above the rest of the flat roof to become a conical tower.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Royal_Exchange_Building.jpg/250px-Royal_Exchange_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Exchange_Building.jpg)

The [Royal Exchange](/wiki/Royal_Exchange,_Manchester "Royal Exchange, Manchester"), whose eponymous theatre is the largest of its kind in the UK

Manchester is a cultural centre for theatre and the performing arts, with several large venues[[220]](#cite_note-225) including the [Manchester Opera House](/wiki/Manchester_Opera_House "Manchester Opera House"), which features large-scale touring shows and West End productions;[[221]](#cite_note-226) the [Palace Theatre](/wiki/Palace_Theatre,_Manchester "Palace Theatre, Manchester"), which despite near-closure in the 1970s is now one of the most successful in the country;[[222]](#cite_note-227) and the [Royal Exchange Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Exchange_Theatre,_Manchester "Royal Exchange Theatre, Manchester") in Manchester's former cotton exchange, the largest [theatre in the round](/wiki/Theatre_in_the_round "Theatre in the round") in the UK.[[223]](#cite_note-228) Smaller venues include the [Contact Theatre](/wiki/Contact_Theatre "Contact Theatre"). The [Dancehouse](/wiki/Dancehouse "Dancehouse") on Oxford Road is dedicated to dance productions.[[224]](#cite_note-229) In 2014, [HOME](/wiki/HOME_(Manchester) "HOME (Manchester)"), an arts complex with two theatre spaces, five cinemas and an art exhibition space, opened; it replaced the [Cornerhouse](/wiki/Cornerhouse "Cornerhouse") and The [Library Theatre](/wiki/Library_Theatre "Library Theatre").[[225]](#cite_note-230) Since 2007, the city has hosted the [Manchester International Festival](/wiki/Manchester_International_Festival "Manchester International Festival"), a biennial international [arts festival](/wiki/Arts_festival "Arts festival") focusing on original work.[[226]](#cite_note-231)

### Museums and galleries

[![A triangular prism shaped glass building as photographed from a short distance away, with trees lined in front.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/National_Football_Museum%2C_Cathedral_Gardens_%28geograph_6944591%29.jpg/250px-National_Football_Museum%2C_Cathedral_Gardens_%28geograph_6944591%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Football_Museum,_Cathedral_Gardens_(geograph_6944591).jpg)

The [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum")

The [Science and Industry Museum](/wiki/Museum_of_Science_and_Industry_(Manchester) "Museum of Science and Industry (Manchester)"), housed in the former [Liverpool Road railway station](/wiki/Liverpool_Road_railway_station_(Manchester) "Liverpool Road railway station (Manchester)"), has a collection of [steam locomotives](/wiki/Steam_locomotives "Steam locomotives"), industrial machinery, aircraft and a replica of [Manchester Baby](/wiki/Manchester_Baby "Manchester Baby"), the world's first stored computer.[[227]](#cite_note-mosi-232) The [Museum of Transport](/wiki/Museum_of_Transport_in_Manchester "Museum of Transport in Manchester") displays a collection of historic buses and trams.[[228]](#cite_note-gmts-233) The [Manchester Museum](/wiki/Manchester_Museum "Manchester Museum"), opened to the public in the 1880s, has [Egyptology](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology") and [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history") collections.[[229]](#cite_note-museum-234) Other local exhibition spaces and museums include the [National Football Museum](/wiki/National_Football_Museum "National Football Museum") at [Urbis](/wiki/Urbis "Urbis"), [Castlefield Gallery](/wiki/Castlefield_Gallery "Castlefield Gallery"), the Manchester Costume Gallery at [Platt Fields Park](/wiki/Platt_Fields_Park "Platt Fields Park"), the [People's History Museum](/wiki/People%27s_History_Museum "People's History Museum") and the [Manchester Jewish Museum](/wiki/Manchester_Jewish_Museum "Manchester Jewish Museum").[[230]](#cite_note-virtualmanc-235) A reconstructed part of the Roman fort of Mamucium is open to the public in [Castlefield](/wiki/Castlefield "Castlefield").[[231]](#cite_note-236)

[![A neopalladian cuboid building as viewed centred on its corner from across the street. Halfway down the facade is a portico, and there is a central cuboid atrium that rises in the centre of the roof above the main roof height. There are regularly arranged sash windows in two stories.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Manchester_Art_Gallery_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1748756.jpg/250px-Manchester_Art_Gallery_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1748756.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manchester_Art_Gallery_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1748756.jpg)

Manchester Art Gallery

The municipally owned [Manchester Art Gallery](/wiki/Manchester_Art_Gallery "Manchester Art Gallery") houses a permanent collection of European painting and one of Britain's main collections of [Pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite_Brotherhood "Pre-Raphaelite Brotherhood") paintings.[[232]](#cite_note-preraph1-237)[[233]](#cite_note-preraph2-238) The [Whitworth Art Gallery](/wiki/Whitworth_Art_Gallery "Whitworth Art Gallery") displays modern art, sculpture and textiles and was voted Museum of the Year in 2015.[[234]](#cite_note-whitworth-239) The work of [Stretford](/wiki/Stretford "Stretford")-born painter [L. S. Lowry](/wiki/L._S._Lowry "L. S. Lowry"), known for "matchstick" paintings of industrial Manchester and Salford, can be seen in the City and Whitworth Manchester galleries.[[235]](#cite_note-lowry-240)

### Literature

[![A small stucco building with sash windows. It is a cube shape with a slightly slanted roof and two stories, as photographed from the ground in front. Trees are seen on either side of the building.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Gaskell_House_Plymouth_Grove_front.JPG/330px-Gaskell_House_Plymouth_Grove_front.JPG)](/wiki/File:Gaskell_House_Plymouth_Grove_front.JPG)

[Gaskell House](/wiki/Gaskell_House "Gaskell House"), where Mrs Gaskell wrote most of her novels. The house is now a museum.

Manchester is a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [City of Literature](/wiki/City_of_Literature "City of Literature") known for a "radical literary history".[[236]](#cite_note-241)[[237]](#cite_note-242) 19th-century works highlighted the changes that industrialisation had brought to the city, including [Elizabeth Gaskell](/wiki/Elizabeth_Gaskell "Elizabeth Gaskell")'s novel *[Mary Barton](/wiki/Mary_Barton "Mary Barton"): A Tale of Manchester Life* (1848),[[238]](#cite_note-243) and [Letitia Landon](/wiki/Letitia_Landon "Letitia Landon")'s poetical illustration *Manchester* (1835).[[239]](#cite_note-244) *[The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1844](/wiki/The_Condition_of_the_Working_Class_in_England_in_1844 "The Condition of the Working Class in England in 1844")* was written about the city by [Friedrich Engels](/wiki/Friedrich_Engels "Friedrich Engels") while resident.[[240]](#cite_note-245) Manchester was the meeting place of Engels and [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx"), where the two began writing *[The Communist Manifesto](/wiki/The_Communist_Manifesto "The Communist Manifesto")* in [Chetham's Library](/wiki/Chetham%27s_Library "Chetham's Library").[[241]](#cite_note-246) The [John Rylands Library](/wiki/John_Rylands_Library "John Rylands Library") holds a collection of early printing including the [Rylands Library Papyrus P52](/wiki/Rylands_Library_Papyrus_P52 "Rylands Library Papyrus P52"), believed to be the earliest extant New Testament text.[[242]](#cite_note-247)

The novel *[Hard Times](/wiki/Hard_Times_(novel) "Hard Times (novel)")* (1854) by [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") is reputed to have been set in Manchester and Preston.[[243]](#cite_note-248) *[Jane Eyre](/wiki/Jane_Eyre "Jane Eyre")* was written by [Charlotte Brontë](/wiki/Charlotte_Bront%C3%AB "Charlotte Brontë") in 1846, while she was staying in her lodgings in [Hulme](/wiki/Hulme "Hulme").[[244]](#cite_note-249) She probably envisioned Manchester Cathedral churchyard as the burial place for Jane's parents and the birthplace of Jane herself.[[245]](#cite_note-250)

Gaskell penned all her novels but *Mary Barton* at her home in [84 Plymouth Grove](/wiki/84_Plymouth_Grove "84 Plymouth Grove"), Manchester. Her house would host influential authors of the time, such as Dickens, Brontë, [Harriet Beecher Stowe](/wiki/Harriet_Beecher_Stowe "Harriet Beecher Stowe") and [Charles Eliot Norton](/wiki/Charles_Eliot_Norton "Charles Eliot Norton").[[246]](#cite_note-Independent-251) [Isabella Banks](/wiki/Isabella_Banks "Isabella Banks") was born in the city and wrote *[The Manchester Man](/wiki/The_Manchester_Man_(novel) "The Manchester Man (novel)")* (1876). Author [Frances Hodgson Burnett](/wiki/Frances_Hodgson_Burnett "Frances Hodgson Burnett") was born in the city's [Cheetham Hill](/wiki/Cheetham_Hill "Cheetham Hill") district in 1849.[[247]](#cite_note-252) [Anthony Burgess](/wiki/Anthony_Burgess "Anthony Burgess") is among the 20th-century writers who lived in Manchester, writing the [dystopian](/wiki/Dystopian "Dystopian") satire *[A Clockwork Orange](/wiki/A_Clockwork_Orange_(novel) "A Clockwork Orange (novel)")* in 1962.[[248]](#cite_note-253) Dame [Carol Ann Duffy](/wiki/Carol_Ann_Duffy "Carol Ann Duffy"), [Poet Laureate](/wiki/Poet_Laureate_of_the_United_Kingdom "Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom") from 2009 to 2019, moved to the city in 1996 and lives in [West Didsbury](/wiki/Didsbury "Didsbury"), a village contiguous within the city.[[249]](#cite_note-254)

### Nightlife

The night-time economy of Manchester has expanded significantly since 1993, with investment from breweries in bars, public houses and clubs, along with active support from the local authorities.[[250]](#cite_note-Park-255) The more than 500 licensed premises[[251]](#cite_note-Hobbs-256) in the city centre have a capacity to serve more than 250,000 visitors,[[252]](#cite_note-257) with 110,000–130,000 people visiting on a typical weekend night,[[251]](#cite_note-Hobbs-256) making Manchester the most popular city for events at 79 per thousand people.[[253]](#cite_note-258) The night-time economy has a value of about £100 million,[[254]](#cite_note-259) and supports 12,000 jobs.[[251]](#cite_note-Hobbs-256) In 2024, Manchester was voted the 8th best city in the world for nightlife, with voters highlighting its variety and inclusivity for different tastes and backgrounds.[[255]](#cite_note-260)

[![A canal as photographed from a bridge over it. On the left hand side there is a brick industrial building that is in shadow; on the right hand side there are brick buildings with many windows facing over the canal, which us lined by brick wall.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Canal_street_manchester.jpg/250px-Canal_street_manchester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canal_street_manchester.jpg)

[Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(Manchester) "Canal Street (Manchester)"), the centre of Manchester's [gay village](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village")

### Gay village

Further information: [Canal Street (Manchester)](/wiki/Canal_Street_(Manchester) "Canal Street (Manchester)")

[Public houses](/wiki/Public_house "Public house") in the [Canal Street](/wiki/Canal_Street_(Manchester) "Canal Street (Manchester)") area have had an LGBTQ+ clientele since at least 1940 and now form the centre of Manchester's LGBTQ+ community and [gay village](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village").[[256]](#cite_note-:14-261)[[257]](#cite_note-Campbell-262) Critics of the area have described it as a "gay ghetto" and that its general popularity has led to a decreased focus on [LGBTQ rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_United_Kingdom "LGBTQ rights in the United Kingdom") and inclusion.[[256]](#cite_note-:14-261) Since the opening of new bars and clubs, the area attracts 20,000 visitors each weekend[[250]](#cite_note-Park-255) and hosted the [Manchester Pride](/wiki/Manchester_Pride "Manchester Pride") festival each August since 1985.[[258]](#cite_note-men2016-263) Despite its high attendance, Manchester Pride received criticism from within the LGBT community dating as far back as 2007 due to its decisions on where to spend its revenue.[[259]](#cite_note-264)

## Education

See also: [List of schools in Manchester](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Manchester "List of schools in Manchester")

### Schooling

[![A black and white pencil drawing of some stone and brick buildings forming a small complex from a short distance away. The buildings each have a door and some windows, with children in the courtyard in front.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/The_First_Manchester_Grammar_School.jpg/250px-The_First_Manchester_Grammar_School.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_First_Manchester_Grammar_School.jpg)

The original building of [Manchester Grammar School](/wiki/Manchester_Grammar_School "Manchester Grammar School")

In 2019, the Manchester [Local Education Authority](/wiki/Local_Education_Authority "Local Education Authority") (LEA) was ranked second to last out of Greater Manchester's ten LEAs and 140th out of 151 in the country LEAs based on the percentage of pupils attaining grades 4 or above in English and mathematics GCSEs ([General Certificate of Secondary Education](/wiki/General_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "General Certificate of Secondary Education")) with 56.2% compared with the national average of 64.9%.[[260]](#cite_note-265) Of the 63 secondary schools in the LEA, four had 80% or more pupils achieving Grade 4 or above in English and maths GCSEs: [Manchester High School for Girls](/wiki/Manchester_High_School_for_Girls "Manchester High School for Girls"), [The King David High School](/wiki/King_David_School,_Manchester "King David School, Manchester"), Manchester Islamic High School for Girls, and Kassim Darwish Grammar School for Boys.[[261]](#cite_note-266) The [Manchester Grammar School](/wiki/Manchester_Grammar_School "Manchester Grammar School"), established in 1515 in the city, is the largest private day school for boys in the United Kingdom.[[262]](#cite_note-Man_GS-267)

### Higher education

[![A grand gothic church-like building as viewed from a short distance away across a pedestrianised street. The building has a grand stained glass window at the front surrounded by two cuboid towers, and a large cuboid tower at the back-right with a red brick roof.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Whitworth_Hall_Manchester.jpg/250px-Whitworth_Hall_Manchester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Whitworth_Hall_Manchester.jpg)

[![The interior of a grand hall as photographed from the back-centre. The hall is full of chairs and has a grand and intricate beamed roof with chandeliers hanging from the beams and gothic-shaped windows either side.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/The_University_Of_Manchester_Whitworth_Hall.jpg/250px-The_University_Of_Manchester_Whitworth_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_University_Of_Manchester_Whitworth_Hall.jpg)

The exterior and interior of the [Whitworth Building](/wiki/Whitworth_Building "Whitworth Building") at the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester")

There are three universities in the City of Manchester: the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester"), [Manchester Metropolitan University](/wiki/Manchester_Metropolitan_University "Manchester Metropolitan University") and the [Royal Northern College of Music](/wiki/Royal_Northern_College_of_Music "Royal Northern College of Music").[[263]](#cite_note-:1-268) The three universities are grouped around Oxford Road on the southern side of the city centre, which forms Europe's largest urban higher-education precinct.[[264]](#cite_note-Higher_edu-269) They have a combined population of over 80,000 students as of 2022.[[263]](#cite_note-:1-268)

The University of Manchester is the second largest full-time non-[collegiate university](/wiki/Collegiate_university "Collegiate university") in the United Kingdom,[[263]](#cite_note-:1-268) and was created in 2004 through the merger of [Victoria University of Manchester](/wiki/Victoria_University_of_Manchester "Victoria University of Manchester") and the [University of Manchester Institute of Science and Technology](/wiki/University_of_Manchester_Institute_of_Science_and_Technology "University of Manchester Institute of Science and Technology").[[265]](#cite_note-Man_Uni-270) The University of Manchester also includes the [Manchester Business School](/wiki/Manchester_Business_School "Manchester Business School"), which offered the first [Master of Business Administration](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") course in the UK in 1965.[[266]](#cite_note-271) It is one of the 24 universities that form the [Russell Group](/wiki/Russell_Group "Russell Group"), having been a founding member in 1994.[[267]](#cite_note-272) The university has been the site of scientific developments: [Ernest Rutherford](/wiki/Ernest_Rutherford "Ernest Rutherford") led a team which first discovered the [nuclear atom](/wiki/Nuclear_atom "Nuclear atom") and inaugurated the beginnings of [nuclear physics](/wiki/Nuclear_physics "Nuclear physics") in 1919;[[268]](#cite_note-273) [Frederic C. Williams](/wiki/Frederic_C._Williams "Frederic C. Williams"), [Tom Kilburn](/wiki/Tom_Kilburn "Tom Kilburn") and [Geoff Tootill](/wiki/Geoff_Tootill "Geoff Tootill") developed the world's first [Stored-program computer](/wiki/Stored-program_computer "Stored-program computer"), the [Manchester Baby](/wiki/Manchester_Baby "Manchester Baby"), in 1948;[[269]](#cite_note-274) and [Andre Geim](/wiki/Andre_Geim "Andre Geim") and [Konstantin Novoselov](/wiki/Konstantin_Novoselov "Konstantin Novoselov") first isolated [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene") in 2004.[[270]](#cite_note-275)

[Manchester Metropolitan University](/wiki/Manchester_Metropolitan_University "Manchester Metropolitan University") was formed as Manchester Polytechnic on the merger of three colleges in 1970. It gained university status in 1992, and in the same year absorbed Crewe and Alsager College of Higher Education in South Cheshire.[[271]](#cite_note-276) The Cheshire campus permanently closed in 2019.[[272]](#cite_note-277) [The University of Law](/wiki/The_University_of_Law "The University of Law"), the largest provider of vocation legal training in Europe, has a campus in the city.[[273]](#cite_note-278)

## Sport

Main article: [Sport in Manchester](/wiki/Sport_in_Manchester "Sport in Manchester")

[![A football stadium as viewed from one corner. The photograph is a fish-eye paorama taken during a game against a blue sky. The stadium is full of fans on all four sides of the pitch, with large stands stretching up below an arched roof.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Etihad_Stadium.jpg/330px-Etihad_Stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Etihad_Stadium.jpg)

The [Etihad Stadium](/wiki/City_of_Manchester_Stadium "City of Manchester Stadium") is home to Premier League club Manchester City F.C. and host stadium for the 2002 Commonwealth Games.

Two [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") clubs bear the city's name – [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C.") and [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.").[[274]](#cite_note-279) Manchester City's home is the [City of Manchester Stadium](/wiki/City_of_Manchester_Stadium "City of Manchester Stadium") in east Manchester, built for the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") and then reconfigured as a football ground in 2003. Manchester United, despite originating in Manchester, have been based in the neighbouring borough of [Trafford](/wiki/Trafford "Trafford") since 1910. Their stadium [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford "Old Trafford") is adjacent to [Lancashire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Lancashire_County_Cricket_Club "Lancashire County Cricket Club") ground, also called [Old Trafford](/wiki/Old_Trafford_(cricket_ground) "Old Trafford (cricket ground)"). The cricket club has strong association with Manchester due to proximity to the city and Manchester historically being part of [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire").[[275]](#cite_note-280)

Sporting facilities built for the [2002 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2002_Commonwealth_Games "2002 Commonwealth Games") include the City of Manchester Stadium, [National Squash Centre](/wiki/National_Squash_Centre "National Squash Centre") and [Manchester Aquatics Centre](/wiki/Manchester_Aquatics_Centre "Manchester Aquatics Centre").[[276]](#cite_note-281) Manchester has competed twice to host the Olympic Games, beaten by [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta") for [1996](/wiki/Bids_for_the_1996_Summer_Olympics "Bids for the 1996 Summer Olympics") and [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney") for [2000](/wiki/Bids_for_the_2000_Summer_Olympics "Bids for the 2000 Summer Olympics"). The [National Cycling Centre](/wiki/National_Cycling_Centre "National Cycling Centre") includes a velodrome, BMX Arena and Mountainbike trials, and is the home of [British Cycling](/wiki/British_Cycling "British Cycling"), UCI ProTeam [Team Sky](/wiki/Team_Sky "Team Sky") and [Sky Track Cycling](/wiki/Sky_Track_Cycling "Sky Track Cycling"). The [Manchester Velodrome](/wiki/Manchester_Velodrome "Manchester Velodrome"), built as a part of the bid for the 2000 games, has become a catalyst for British success in cycling.[[250]](#cite_note-Park-255)

The velodrome hosted the [UCI Track Cycling World Championships](/wiki/UCI_Track_Cycling_World_Championships "UCI Track Cycling World Championships") for a record third time in 2008. The [National Indoor BMX Arena](/wiki/National_Indoor_BMX_Arena "National Indoor BMX Arena") (2,000 capacity) adjacent to the velodrome opened in 2011. The [Manchester Arena](/wiki/Manchester_Arena "Manchester Arena") hosted the [FINA](/wiki/FINA "FINA") World Swimming Championships in 2008.[[277]](#cite_note-FINA-282) Manchester hosted the 2008 [World Squash Championships](/wiki/World_Open_(squash) "World Open (squash)"),[[278]](#cite_note-283) the [2010 World Lacrosse Championship](/wiki/2010_World_Lacrosse_Championship "2010 World Lacrosse Championship"),[[279]](#cite_note-284) the [2013 Ashes series](/wiki/2013_Ashes_series "2013 Ashes series"),[[280]](#cite_note-2012-2016_Internationals-285) the [2013 Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/2013_Rugby_League_World_Cup "2013 Rugby League World Cup"),[[281]](#cite_note-286) the [2015 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2015_Rugby_World_Cup "2015 Rugby World Cup"),[[282]](#cite_note-287) the [2019 Ashes series](/wiki/2019_Ashes_series "2019 Ashes series"),[[283]](#cite_note-288) and the [2019 Cricket World Cup](/wiki/2019_Cricket_World_Cup "2019 Cricket World Cup").[[284]](#cite_note-289)

## Media

Main article: [Media in Manchester](/wiki/Media_in_Manchester "Media in Manchester")

See also: [List of television programmes set, produced or filmed in Manchester](/wiki/List_of_television_programmes_set,_produced_or_filmed_in_Manchester "List of television programmes set, produced or filmed in Manchester"); [Films set in Manchester](/wiki/Films_set_in_Manchester "Films set in Manchester"); and [List of national radio programmes made in Manchester](/wiki/List_of_national_radio_programmes_made_in_Manchester "List of national radio programmes made in Manchester")

### Print

[![An art deco glass and steel building that is largely built from cuboids joined together, some of which have rounded corners. The photograph is taken on a sunny day from across the street.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Express_Building_Manchester.jpg/250px-Express_Building_Manchester.jpg)](/wiki/File:Express_Building_Manchester.jpg)

The 1930s [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco#Europe "Art Deco") [Express Building](/wiki/Daily_Express_Building,_Manchester "Daily Express Building, Manchester") on [Great Ancoats Street](/wiki/Great_Ancoats_Street "Great Ancoats Street"), a remnant of Britain's "second Fleet Street"

*[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* newspaper was founded in the city in 1821 as *The Manchester Guardian*. Until 2008, its head office was still in the city, though many of its management functions were moved to London in 1964.[[35]](#cite_note-Kidd-39)[[285]](#cite_note-guardian-timeline-290) For many years most national newspapers had offices in Manchester: *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*, *[Daily Express](/wiki/Daily_Express "Daily Express")*, *[Daily Mail](/wiki/Daily_Mail "Daily Mail")*, *[Daily Mirror](/wiki/Daily_Mirror "Daily Mirror")*, *[The Sun](/wiki/The_Sun_(United_Kingdom) "The Sun (United Kingdom)")*. At its height, 1,500 journalists were employed, earning the city the nickname "second [Fleet Street](/wiki/Fleet_Street "Fleet Street")". In the 1980s the titles closed their northern offices and centred their operations in London.[[286]](#cite_note-291)

An attempt to launch a Northern daily newspaper, the *North West Times*, employing journalists made redundant by other titles, closed in 1988.[[287]](#cite_note-newpapers-292) Another attempt was made with the *[North West Enquirer](/wiki/North_West_Enquirer "North West Enquirer")*, which hoped to provide a true "regional" newspaper for the [North West](/wiki/North_West_England "North West England"), much in the same vein as the *[Yorkshire Post](/wiki/Yorkshire_Post "Yorkshire Post")* does for [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire") or *[The Northern Echo](/wiki/The_Northern_Echo "The Northern Echo")* does for the [North East](/wiki/North_East_England "North East England"); it folded in October 2006.[[287]](#cite_note-newpapers-292)

The main regional newspaper in the city is the *[Manchester Evening News](/wiki/Manchester_Evening_News "Manchester Evening News")*, which was for over 80 years the sister publication of *The Manchester Guardian*.[[285]](#cite_note-guardian-timeline-290) The *Manchester Evening News* has the largest circulation of a UK regional evening newspaper and is distributed free of charge in the city centre on Thursdays and Fridays, but paid for in the suburbs. Despite its title, it is available all day.[[288]](#cite_note-293) Several local weekly free papers are distributed by the MEN group. The *[Metro](/wiki/Metro_(Associated_Metro_Limited) "Metro (Associated Metro Limited)") North West* is available free at [Metrolink](/wiki/Manchester_Metrolink "Manchester Metrolink") stops, rail stations and other busy locations.[[289]](#cite_note-294)

### Television

[![A glass and steel building that is a cuboid shape with a perpendicular single-storey extension sticking out towards the camera. The side of this extension closest to the camera is decorated with leaves and has a white sign that read "GRANADA / TV"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Granada_TV.jpg/250px-Granada_TV.jpg)](/wiki/File:Granada_TV.jpg)

[Granada Studios](/wiki/Granada_Studios "Granada Studios"), the former headquarters of [Granada Television](/wiki/Granada_Television "Granada Television")

Manchester has been a centre of [television broadcasting](/wiki/Television_in_the_United_Kingdom "Television in the United Kingdom") since the 1950s, with several television studios in operation.[[290]](#cite_note-295) The [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") franchise [Granada Television](/wiki/ITV_Granada "ITV Granada") has been based in Manchester since 1954. Now based at [MediaCityUK](/wiki/MediaCityUK "MediaCityUK"), the company's former headquarters at [Granada Studios](/wiki/Granada_Studios "Granada Studios") on [Quay Street](/wiki/Quay_Street "Quay Street") with its distinctive illuminated sign were a prominent landmark on the Manchester skyline for several decades.[[291]](#cite_note-296)[[292]](#cite_note-Skillset-297)[[293]](#cite_note-298) Granada produces *[Coronation Street](/wiki/Coronation_Street "Coronation Street")*,[[294]](#cite_note-299) local news and programmes for North West England.[[295]](#cite_note-300)[[296]](#cite_note-301) Manchester is also covered by an internet television channel called Manchester TV.[[297]](#cite_note-302)

With the growth in regional television in the 1950s, Manchester became one of the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")'s three main centres in England.[[292]](#cite_note-Skillset-297) In 1954, the BBC opened its first regional [BBC Television](/wiki/BBC_Television "BBC Television") studio outside London, [Dickenson Road Studios](/wiki/Dickenson_Road_Studios "Dickenson Road Studios"), in a converted Methodist chapel in [Rusholme](/wiki/Rusholme "Rusholme"). The first edition of *[Top of the Pops](/wiki/Top_of_the_Pops "Top of the Pops")* was broadcast there on New Year's Day 1964.[[298]](#cite_note-TOTP-303)[[299]](#cite_note-itsahotun-history-304) From 1975, BBC programmes including *[Mastermind](/wiki/Mastermind_(television) "Mastermind (television)")*[[300]](#cite_note-BBC_programs-305) and *[Real Story](/wiki/Real_Story "Real Story")*[[301]](#cite_note-BBC_real_story-306) were made at [New Broadcasting House](/wiki/New_Broadcasting_House_(Manchester) "New Broadcasting House (Manchester)") on [Oxford Road](/wiki/Oxford_Road,_Manchester "Oxford Road, Manchester"). *[Cutting It](/wiki/Cutting_It "Cutting It")* and *[The Street](/wiki/The_Street_(BBC_series) "The Street (BBC series)")* were set in Manchester,[[302]](#cite_note-307) as was *[Life on Mars](/wiki/Life_on_Mars_(UK_TV_series) "Life on Mars (UK TV series)")*. Manchester was the regional base for [BBC One](/wiki/BBC_One "BBC One") North West Region programmes before it relocated to MediaCityUK.[[303]](#cite_note-Media_city-308)

### Radio

As of 2016, Manchester has 10 licensed radio stations, which is the joint-fourth highest in the UK; the city is beaten only by London, Glasgow, and Birmingham, and ties with Cardiff and Edinburgh.[[304]](#cite_note-309) Local radio stations include [BBC Radio Manchester](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Manchester "BBC Radio Manchester"), [Hits Radio Manchester](/wiki/Hits_Radio_Manchester "Hits Radio Manchester"), [Capital Manchester and Lancashire](/wiki/Capital_Manchester_and_Lancashire "Capital Manchester and Lancashire"), [Greatest Hits Radio Manchester & The North West](/wiki/Greatest_Hits_Radio_Manchester_%26_The_North_West "Greatest Hits Radio Manchester & The North West"), [Heart North West](/wiki/Heart_North_West "Heart North West"), [Smooth North West](/wiki/Smooth_North_West "Smooth North West"), [Gold](/wiki/Gold_(British_radio_network) "Gold (British radio network)"), and [Radio X](/wiki/Radio_X_(United_Kingdom) "Radio X (United Kingdom)").[[305]](#cite_note-ofcomradio-310)

Student radio stations include [Fuse FM](/wiki/Fuse_FM "Fuse FM") at the University of Manchester and MMU Radio at the Manchester Metropolitan University.[[306]](#cite_note-311) A [community radio](/wiki/Community_radio "Community radio") network is coordinated by Radio Regen, with stations covering [Ardwick](/wiki/Ardwick "Ardwick"), [Longsight](/wiki/Longsight "Longsight") and [Levenshulme](/wiki/Levenshulme "Levenshulme") ([All FM](/wiki/All_FM "All FM") 96.9) and [Wythenshawe](/wiki/Wythenshawe "Wythenshawe") (Wythenshawe FM 97.2).[[305]](#cite_note-ofcomradio-310)

## International relations

The expansion of international trade links during the Industrial Revolution led to the introduction of the first consuls in the 1820s and since then over 800, from all parts of the world, have been based in Manchester. Manchester hosts consular services for most of the north of England.[[307]](#cite_note-:19-312)

### Sister cities

Manchester is [twinned](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") with [Chemnitz](/wiki/Chemnitz "Chemnitz"), Germany since 1983[[308]](#cite_note-313) and [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan"), China since 1986.[[309]](#cite_note-314)[[310]](#cite_note-intstrategy-315) [Greater Manchester](/wiki/Greater_Manchester "Greater Manchester") is twinned with [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"), Japan, since 2025[[311]](#cite_note-316) and also cooperates with numerous other cities.[[310]](#cite_note-intstrategy-315)

### Friendship agreements

In addition to its sister cities, Manchester has friendly relations with:[[310]](#cite_note-intstrategy-315)

* [Aalborg](/wiki/Aalborg "Aalborg"), Denmark
* [Aarhus](/wiki/Aarhus "Aarhus"), Denmark
* [Córdoba](/wiki/C%C3%B3rdoba,_Spain "Córdoba, Spain"), Spain
* [Faisalabad](/wiki/Faisalabad "Faisalabad"), Pakistan (1997)
* [Gumi](/wiki/Gumi,_North_Gyeongsang "Gumi, North Gyeongsang"), South Korea
* [Haidian (Beijing)](/wiki/Haidian,_Beijing "Haidian, Beijing"), China
* [Kagoshima](/wiki/Kagoshima "Kagoshima"), Japan
* [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), United States (2009)
* [Rehovot](/wiki/Rehovot "Rehovot"), Israel

### Diplomatic missions

Manchester has the largest group of [consulates](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") in England outside London.[[307]](#cite_note-:19-312)[[f]](#cite_note-320) The current consulate-generals, unless otherwise noted, are:

* Bangladesh (Assistant High Commission)[[315]](#cite_note-321)
* China ([Consulate](/wiki/Consulate_General_of_China,_Manchester "Consulate General of China, Manchester"))[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)
* Czech Republic[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[317]](#cite_note-323)
* Greece[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[318]](#cite_note-MFAGr-UK-324)
* Hungary[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[319]](#cite_note-325)
* India[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[320]](#cite_note-India-326)
* Iraq[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[321]](#cite_note-327)
* Ireland[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[322]](#cite_note-CG_Manchester_Opened-328)
* Italy (Consulate)[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[323]](#cite_note-ItalyCon-Manchester-329)
* Libya[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)
* Pakistan[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[324]](#cite_note-phclondon.org-330)
* Poland[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[325]](#cite_note-gov.pl-331)
* Portugal[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)
* Romania[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)[[326]](#cite_note-332)
* Spain[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)
* Turkey[[316]](#cite_note-GovUK-EmbInUK-322)
